segments.s181-g217: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed frontally to report speech. گفت is the past tense verb 'said'; the natural order here is verb + subject (گفت رستم) as is common in epic Persian. به + شاه زمین is a prepositional phrase 'to the king of the land' (شاه زمین = 'king of the land/country'), with به marking the addressee.
segments.s181-g218: که‌ای here functions as an archaic/vocative combination (که + ای) addressing someone — roughly 'O' or 'O thou'. نامبردار is an adjective/noun meaning 'renowned, famed'. باآفرین literally 'with praise' is an epithet attached after the name, i.e. 'worthy of praise' or 'praised one.'
segments.s181-g219: در introduces the locative phrase 'in Bazavolstan'; یکی شهر is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain city' (یکی functioning as 'a/certain'), and بود is the past-tense existential/copula 'there was'. The overall order (place + 'in' + noun + بود) yields 'There was in Bazavolstan a certain city.'
segments.s181-g220: کزان is a relative/locative particle 'from which' referring back to that city. بوم و بر is a fixed paired phrase meaning 'homeland and domain/land' (bôm o bar). تور را بهر بود is a light impersonal/possessive construction: X را بهر بودن = 'belonged to X' or 'was for X', so the clause says the land and domain 'belonged to Tur.'
segments.s181-g221: کرد is the past tense of کردن 'to make/do' and takes آن ('it/that') as its object: 'Manuchehr made that ...' followed by ز + ترکان + تهی meaning 'empty of the Turks.' The construction منوچهر کرد ... ز ترکان تهی = 'Manuchehr made it void/cleared it of the Turks.'
segments.s181-g222: یکی is the indefinite determiner 'a certain' modifying جای; خوب qualifies جای as 'good/beautiful.' جایست is a fused predicate 'it is a place / it is situated' (جای + است), here asserting the city's quality. با + فرهی attaches an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with nobility/glory' describing the place's character.
segments.s181-g223: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when.' شد is the intransitive past 'became,' taking the following adjectives as result states: بی‌دل 'heartless/without courage' and پیرسر 'old/gray‑headed.' The clause therefore reads 'When Kavus became faint‑hearted and old.'
segments.s181-g224: بیفتاد ازو is an idiomatic passive/impersonal phrase 'fell from him' or 'departed from him.' نام شاهی here refers to 'the name/title/reputation of kingship' and فر is the technical epic term for royal glory; the coordinate phrase نام شاهی و فر is the thing that 'fell from him.'
segments.s181-g121: This phrase states possession: پرستنده‌ای (an indefinite 'a worshipper/devoted follower') is the thing possessed. دارد is the present-tense verb 'has' (present simple). او is the possessor 'he'. روز جنگ is a time phrase 'the day of battle' placed after the possessed noun for poetic word order.
segments.s181-g122: کز introduces a relative/clausal modifier ('who/by whom'); here it means 'by whose...'. آواز او is the possessed noun phrase 'his voice/call'. رام گردد is a verbal phrase in which گردد (become) is used impersonally/subjunctively to mean 'is/comes to be tamed'. پلنگ is the subject of that clause 'the leopard'. The whole clause modifies the پرستنده‌ای before it.
segments.s181-g123: به رخ is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/with respect to the face' (literally 'to the face'). چون + بهار is a simile 'like spring'. و links the next simile: به بالا چو سرو = 'and in elevation/height like a cypress.' Poetic order places the similes after the prepositional phrase.
segments.s181-g124: میانش is میان + enclitic -ش 'his/its middle'. چو غرو gives another simile 'like twilight/dusk' describing that middle. و begins the next clause; به رفتن uses the infinitive رفتن with به to indicate 'in going/when he goes'; تذرو is a poetic/present-form verb 'scatters/disperses' describing what happens in motion. Together this gives two linked images: middle like twilight, and in motion he scatters (something).
segments.s181-g125: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one'. ماهرویست is a fused adjective/copula meaning 'is moon‑faced' (very beautiful). نام + اسپنوی is a name phrase 'named Aspnavī' (name following the predicate in poetic order).
segments.s181-g126: سمن پیکر is a binomial epithet 'jasmine-bodied' (سمن modifying پیکر). و links attributes: دلبر 'darling/beauty' is a nominative/attributive noun; و مشک بوی is a compound meaning 'and (of) musk-scent' or 'and scented with musk' where بوی (scent) follows مشک (musk). The sequence lists physical and fragrant attributes.
segments.s181-g127: نباید + زدن is an impersonal periphrastic construction meaning 'one must not strike' or 'it is not proper to strike.' چون here introduces a temporal/circumstantial clause 'when/since' and بیابدش is بیابد (3rd-person present 'finds/encounters') with the enclitic -ش 'him/it' (so 'when the sword meets him'). تیغ is the agent/subject 'the sword' placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s181-g128: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. از تیغ باشد uses از + noun to indicate source/agent 'from the sword' and باشد is the subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used here in a predictive/explanatory sense 'there will be' or 'there is.' چنان رخ دریغ is a nominal phrase 'such a face of دریغ' — رخ 'face/appearance' and دریغ an abstract noun meaning pity/reluctance; together they describe the resulting expression.
segments.s181-g377: گزیده is a past/passive participle 'the chosen/selected (one)'. پس here is a discourse particle 'then/thereupon' linking the narrative. اندر + ش (اندرش) means 'among them / in their midst' with ش = 'his/their' referring back to the group; بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole segment literally says 'Chosen then among them was Farhad.'
segments.s181-g378: کزو = 'from whom / by whom' (relates back to Farhad). لشکر خسرو = 'Khosrow's host/army' (possessive noun phrase). آباد بود is an adjectival/predicative phrase 'was made prosperous/was flourishing' (آباد used as predicate). Together: 'by whom Khosrow's army was made prosperous.'
segments.s181-g379: سپه را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for the army'). بکردار is an adjective 'of good conduct/virtuous', and پروردگار here functions as 'lord/protector/commander' (the substantive linked to that adjective). Together: 'As for the army, (it had) a virtuous commander.'
segments.s181-g380: بهر جای = 'for every post/place/office' (بهر = 'for', جای = 'place/position'). بودی is the past copula 'was' (agreeing with the commander implied in previous segment). به هر کار یار = 'a companion/ally in every affair' (به = 'in/with respect to', هر کار = 'every action/affair', یار = 'companion/ally'). The clause as a whole attributes suitability and helpfulness to that leader.
segments.s181-g381: یکی = 'a certain / one (man)'. پیکرآهو is a compound adjective 'gazelle‑bodied / of gazelle form' describing the person or device. درفش = 'banner/standard', and از برش literally 'from his side' (از = 'from/of', برش here means 'his side/part/side/portion'). The phrase describes the banner carried by that one.
segments.s181-g382: بدان here functions as 'with that' / 'on it' (classical contraction of به + آن) introducing the following emblem; سایه آهو = 'the shadow/likeness of a gazelle' (a poetic way to name an image or motif); اندر سرش = 'upon his head' (اندر = 'in/on', سر = 'head', ش = 'his'). The segment says that the gazelle motif was displayed on his head (i.e., on his helmet or crest).
segments.s181-g383: سپاهش = 'his army/host' (سپاه + possessive ش). همه = 'all' (everyone of them). تیغ هندی بدست = 'Indian swords in hand' (تیغ هندی = 'Indian blades/swords', بدست = 'in hand/in their hands'). The clause states the weaponry carried by the troops.
segments.s181-g384: زره سغدی = 'Sogdian armor' (زره = 'armor', سغدی qualifying it). و زین ترکی = 'and Turkish saddle' (زین = 'saddle', ترکی = 'Turkish'). نشست here is a past/perfective verb 'sat/settled' used idiomatically to mean they were equipped or put on these items (i.e., they sat in those saddles/wore that armor).
segments.s181-g257: Two adjectival present‑participle + noun pairs linked by و: غریونده نای = 'the wailing/roaring ney' and خروشنده چنگ = 'the clamouring/roaring harp'. The participial forms (غریونده, خروشنده) precede the noun they modify, a common poetic order, and و simply coordinates the two image phrases.
segments.s181-g258: بدست + اندرون forms a locative phrase 'in the hands of those within/inside' (بدست = 'in the hand(s)'; اندرون = 'inside/within'). دسته بوی و رنگ is a noun phrase head (دسته = 'bundle/bouquet') followed by a coordinating genitive-like description 'of scent and colour' (بوی و رنگ). The whole phrase describes what is held in the inner hands.
segments.s181-g259: A verbless predicative clause listing two qualities with repetition for emphasis: همه تازه‌روی و همه شاددل = 'all fresh‑faced and all cheerful.' Here همه is distributive/emphatic 'all/everyone' and تازه‌روی and شاددل are adjectives/nouns describing people.
segments.s181-g260: ز marks cause/source ('from/because of'), so ز درد و غمان = 'from pain and sorrows.' گشته is the past participle/resultative 'has become' or 'became,' and آزاددل is the resulting predicate 'free‑hearted / free‑spirited.' The clause thus says they became free‑hearted as a result of being freed from pain and sorrow.
segments.s181-g261: ز هرگونه گفتارها راندند is a simple past transitive clause: ز here introduces the object phrase (poetically 'all kinds of speech'), هرگونه modifies گفتارها ('every kind of utterance'), and راندند is the past plural 'they drove away' (verb + -ند plural ending).
segments.s181-g262: سخنهای شاهان is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the words/sayings of the kings.' بسی here is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'much/many' modifying خواندند, which is the past plural 'they recited/read.' The clause thus states they recited many royal sayings.
segments.s181-g263: This relative/subordinate clause begins with که introducing 'that/whoever.' هر کس که = 'whoever/anyone who.' در شاهی او = 'in his kingship/under his rule' (شاهی = 'kingship'). The doubled داد داد is noun + verb: the first داد = 'justice' (object/concept) and the second داد = past tense 'gave' (or here general 'gives'); classical Persian often stacks the noun and verb like this.
segments.s181-g264: شود is the modal/resultive verb 'become' / 'will be' (here indicating the resulting state). در دو گیتی = 'in the two worlds' (i.e., this world and the next), ز کردار = 'because of/from the deeds,' and شاد = 'happy.' Word order places the predicate adjective at the end: 'will be happy in both worlds due to his deeds.'
segments.s181-g65: به پیش فسیله کمند افگنند is a compact, somewhat archaic/poetic clause: به + پیش gives the adverbial sense “forward / before,” فسیله functions as a fronting noun (here 'in ranks/interval/line' in poetic use), کمند is the direct object 'lasso/snare', and افگنند is an archaic 3rd-person plural verb 'they cast/throw'. Word order (adverbial phrase before object then verb) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s181-g66: سر بادپایان به بند افگنند places the patient (سر, 'head') before the agent phrase for emphasis; بادپایان (the swift-footed) is the genitive/qualifier of سر. به بند افگنند uses به + بند ('into/into fetters') plus the same 3rd-person plural verb افگنند 'they put/cast', so the clause means 'they put the heads of the swift-footed into bonds'. The object-first order and the plural verb are typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s181-g67: در گنج دینار بگشاد و گفت is a short narrative clause in past tense: در + گنج دینار 'in the treasure (of dinars)' gives the location/thing; بگشاد is the past or perfective form 'opened' (بـ- prefix + گشاد), و connects actions, and گفت here functions as the reported-speech verb 'said' (a standard narrative verb following the past action).
segments.s181-g68: که گنج از بزرگان نشاید نهفت begins with the complementizer که 'that', introducing reported content. گنج is the subject 'treasure', از بزرگان 'from the great/foremost' marks whose concern it is, نشاید is an impersonal/formal present-potential 'it is not fitting/ought not' (archaic register), and نهفت is a past participle/adjective 'hidden'. Together: 'that treasure ought not to be hidden from the great.'
segments.s181-g69: گه بخشش و کینه شهریار is a nominal phrase: گه 'sometimes' (adverb) sets an occasional frame, بخشش 'giving/grant' and کینه 'grudge/hatred' are coordinated by و, and شهریار 'the king' attaches to both as possessor/agent. The phrase lists contrasting royal acts (generosity and spite) as causes in the following clause.
segments.s181-g70: شود گنج دینار بر چشم‌خوار uses شود (third-person singular subjunctive/impersonal auxiliary here rendered 'becomes' or 'falls') with گنج دینار as subject. بر + چشم‌خوار ('upon/the covetous') indicates the beneficiary/receiver or the target of that change: 'the treasure becomes (subject) to the covetous'. This is a typical Persian construction for 'fall into the hands of' or 'become exposed to' someone.
segments.s181-g71: به مردان همی گنج و تخت آوریم has به مردان 'to/for men' as indirect object, همی + آوردیم (آوریم here) is a poetic present/optative marker meaning 'we shall / we will (habitually) bring'; گنج و تخت are the direct objects 'treasure and throne'. The 1st‑person plural آوریم functions as an optative/future in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s181-g72: به خورشید بار درخت آوریم parallels the previous clause: به خورشید 'to/for the sun' is the indirect object, بار درخت is a nominal phrase 'the load/burden of the tree' or 'a tree bearing a load', and آوریم is the same 1st-person plural optative/future verb 'we will bring'. The sequence is poetic parallelism: we bring X to men; we bring a loaded tree to the sun.
segments.s181-g401: همه (subject) + برگذشتند (past plural verb) form the main clause 'they all passed by/through.' زیر همای is a prepositional phrase ('under/beneath the Homa/royal standard') placed after the verb for poetic word order; همای is the mythical/royal emblem here rather than a personal name.
segments.s181-g402: سپهبد is the subject of the following descriptive clause; همی داشت is an archaic/perfective-continuous construction ('was holding / occupied') where همی gives durative flavour and داشت is the main past verb. بر پیل جای is a locative phrase 'a place/seat on the elephant' (بر = on, جای = place) completing the verb.
segments.s181-g403: بسی زنگه is an adverbial phrase ('many a ringing/peals' — بسی = numerous/poetic intensifier, زنگه = ringing/peal). بر شاه is the indirect object 'to the king' (بر = toward/to), کرد is the past verb 'made/did' and آفرین is the noun object 'acclamation' so together 'many peals made acclamation to the king' = 'many cries hailed the king.'
segments.s181-g404: بران (= 'thereupon' / 'upon him') points back to the king; the following nouns (برز و بالا و تیغ و نگین) are a linked list of praised or conferred attributes: برز (exaltation/high rank), بالا (loftiness/high position), تیغ (the sword) and نگین (a gem/signet). Word order is a poetic noun list joined by و.
segments.s181-g405: ز پشت here is a locative/positional phrase 'from the rear' or 'at the back'; سپهبد فرامرز is the noun phrase 'Spahbed Faramarz' placed after the locative, and بود is the past copula 'was/stood' completing the clause (typical classical word order puts the verb at the end).
segments.s181-g406: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; the repeated با + noun pattern ('با فر و با گرز و باارز') lists possessions/attributes he carried: فر (farr, royal glory), گرز (mace), باارز (of value/esteemed). The final بود is the copula 'was' tying these attributes to him.
segments.s181-g407: This is a prepositional list introduced by (poetic) با ('with') — here written ابا in the manuscript — followed by items joined by و: کوس (war-drums), پیل (elephants), سپاهی گران ('heavy/weighty troops' = mighty forces). The phrase sets the military display or accompaniment.
segments.s181-g408: همه modifies the following coordinated nouns: رزم جویان is a compound 'seekers of battle' (warriors eager for combat) and کنداوران are 'diggers/excavators' (here meaning sappers or siege-workers). The whole phrase enumerates the kinds of men present.
segments.s181-g297: یکی (a single) precedes the noun مهره (bead/amulet); the locative phrases در جام (in the cup) and بر دست شاه (on the king's hand) come after the noun, stacking modifiers in the usual Persian order. The whole phrase describes the position of one bead as located in the cup that is on the king's hand.
segments.s181-g298: خروش سپاه (the army's roar) is the subject and رسیده (arrived/reached) is a past participle/predicate; in this poetic order بکیوان (to Keyvān) follows the verb and names the destination reached. Read as “the army's cry has reached Keyvān.”
segments.s181-g299: چو here means 'when'. بر پشت پیل is a prepositional phrase 'on the elephant's back', and آن شه نامور (that famed king) is the person located there — the time clause puts the scene-setting element (when/on the elephant) before the main action, a common poetic order.
segments.s181-g300: زدی is a past action (classical/epic past form) meaning 'he struck/placed' or 'he set'; مهره بر جام is the object + locative ('the bead in/on the cup'); و links the two verbs, with بستی (he fastened) taking کمر (waist/girdle) as object — 'basti kamar' is the idiom 'girded his loins/belt'.
segments.s181-g301: نبودی is a past negative copula meaning 'was not' (classical/poetic), and بهر پادشاهی marks the beneficiary/affected domain ('for kingship' / 'for a kingly office'). روا is the predicate meaning 'fitting' or 'proper' — together: 'it was not fitting for kingship.'
segments.s181-g302: نشستن is the infinitive/noun 'to sit' (the action judged); مگر is 'except'; بر در پادشا = 'upon/at the monarch's gate/threshold' — note پادشا is a poetic/contracted form of پادشاه. The clause reads 'to sit was not fitting except at the king's gate.'
segments.s181-g303: ازان ('from that') links back to the preceding scene; نامور خسرو is an apposition 'that famed Khosrow'. سرکشان appears as a following noun (probably plural) attached to Khosrow, but its precise sense/inflexion is ambiguous here (see note). Grammatically the phrase names agents or a group associated with Khosrow.
segments.s181-g304: چنین بود ('thus was') is a formulaic predicate; در پادشاهی is a locative/administrative phrase 'in the kingship/realm', and نشان is 'sign' or 'token' — together 'thus was a sign/mark in the kingdom,' i.e. this was a distinguishing token of his rule.
segments.s181-g25: فروزنده is a present-participial epithet (agentive) placed before the nouns it qualifies; here it functions as a title 'illuminating/illuminator'. The coordinated nouns تاج و تخت form a single nominal phrase 'crown and throne' (تاج and تخت joined by the conjunction و), and کیان after them is the genitive/attributive target: 'of the Kians' or 'of the Kayanian house.' Word order is noun phrase (epithet + coordinated nouns + possessor).
segments.s181-g26: فرازنده is another agentive epitheton (here 'exalter' or 'one who raises/exalts') used appositionally; اختر کاویان is a nominal phrase 'the star of the Kayanians' where اختر ('star') is modified by the following proper-name genitive کاویان (Kayanian dynasty). The two halves form parallel epithets describing the same lofty figure.
segments.s181-g27: چو here functions temporally 'when' introducing a clause describing a number: شصت و سه (sixty‑three) is written with an internal و joining the tens and units (شصت و سه). از تخمه ... is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of/from the seed (offspring) of ...'; تخمه carries the meaning 'offspring/lineage' and گژدهم is a proper name in genitive position.
segments.s181-g28: This small clause lists the persons: بزرگان 'the great ones' (elders/leading men) coordinated with سالارشان 'their leader/commander.' گستهم is an archaic/poetic first‑person verb ('I wandered' / 'I ranged'); it is a finite past‑tense form in the first person, so the speaker is stating his own action after mentioning those men.
segments.s181-g29: ز is the poetic form of 'from' introducing origin. خویشان میلاد 'kinsmen's birth' is a genitive construction; بد modifies میلاد and here carries the sense 'ill/unfavorable' (an ill‑omened or unlucky birth). صد سوار is a numeral phrase 'a hundred horsemen' where سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman.' The clause omits an explicit verb (a common poetic compression), implying 'there were' or 'came forth.'
segments.s181-g30: چو here functions as a comparative/simile 'like' (or 'as'), introducing the example/person compared. گرگین is a proper name (Gorgin) followed by two epithets: پیروزگر 'victor' (an agentive adjective/noun) and مایه‌دار 'wealthy.' The order is name then attributive descriptors (name + epithets).
segments.s181-g31: ز تخم X 'from the seed/stock of X' marks origin; تخم here is a noun meaning 'seed/origin' and لواده is treated as a proper name (name of a stock/place). چو هشتادو is a temporal/quantitative construction where چو 'when' precedes the numeral 'eighty‑five' (هشتادو + implicit 'پنج' understood as part of numbering); overall the phrase conveys 'there were eighty‑five from that stock.'
segments.s181-g32: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' followed by coordinating descriptive nouns: رزم (battle/war) and نگهبان گنج 'guard(ians) of the treasure.' The sequence names their role (combatants) and duty (treasure‑keepers); word order places the general noun first and then the coordinating modifiers.
segments.s181-g97: به + noun marks the indirect object/dative — “to the treasurer.” فرمود is the past/polite verb ‘he said/ordered.’ پس is temporal ‘then.’ شهریار is the subject ‘the king’; word order is inverted (verb before subject) because of the poem’s syntax.
segments.s181-g98: که introduces a reported or subordinate clause (’that/so that’). آرد here is a third‑person present/jussive form used in indirect command — ‘bring / should bring.’ دو صد is the numeral ‘two hundred,’ جامه is ‘garment/robe,’ and زرنگار is an adjective/noun meaning ‘brocaded/gilded’ describing the garments.
segments.s181-g99: صد + از indicates a count/partitive: ‘one hundred of (made) from…’. Here خز and دیبا name the materials/types (velvet/fur and brocade). The second صد repeats the count for پرنیان (silken robes).
segments.s181-g100: دو is ‘two.’ گلرخ is used as a noun/adjective ‘rose‑faced’ (literally ‘Golrukh’/‘flower‑faced’) describing two items. به زنار بسته is a participial phrase ‘bound/tied with a girdle’ (بسته = bound, passive/participial use). میان here specifies position ‘in the middle / between.’
segments.s181-g101: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase ‘thus said.’ کین here functions as a demonstrative/construction meaning ‘that (this)’ in classical/poetic usage (an older blend of که + این); هدیه آن را places هدیه ‘gift’ as the direct object (آن = ‘that’), with را the definite object marker. The clause means ‘He said: I give that gift.’
segments.s181-g102: وزان پس = ‘and/from that time then’ (وزان = from that/thereafter). بدو is dative ‘to him.’ نیز دیِگر together mean ‘also another/again’ (نیز = also, دیگر = another). دهم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive/polite form ‘I give / I will give.’
segments.s181-g103: که again introduces the subordinate clause ‘that…’. تاج تژاو is a noun phrase: تاج ‘crown’ modified by تژاو ‘radiant/bright’ (post‑nominal adjective or epithet common in poetry). آورد is a third‑person form meaning ‘bring’ (here used as jussive/commanded action). پیش من = ‘before me / in my presence.’
segments.s181-g104: وگر is ‘or if / but if’ introducing an alternative. پیش این نامدار انجمن = ‘in the presence of this renowned assembly’ (این = this; نامدار = renowned; انجمن = assembly/chapel/assembly). The clause offers a second place where the crown might be presented.
segments.s181-g73: چرا is an interrogative adverb 'why'. برد here is a poetic/elliptical verbal element meaning 'to bear / to take on' used with the modal بايد to express obligation (poetry often omits auxiliaries or uses older verb forms). بايد is the modal 'must/ought'. غم روزگار is a noun phrase 'sorrow of fate/times' (the ezāfe linking is omitted in poetry), serving as the object of the verbal idea 'to bear'.
segments.s181-g74: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/since'). گنج is 'treasure'. از پی is a prepositional phrase 'after, following' (literally 'from after'), with مردم 'the people' as its object. آید is 'comes' and the phrase آید به کار ('comes to work') means 'comes into use' or 'is put to work/used'.
segments.s181-g75: بزرگان ایران is a genitive noun phrase 'the great ones of Iran' (nobles). از انجمن literally 'from the assembly' functions idiomatically as 'in (the) assembly' or 'at the council'—چِنین source-marking with از is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s181-g76: نشسته is a participle/adjectival-predicate 'seated'. به پیشش = 'in his presence / before him' where پیشش is 'پیش' + 3rd-person suffix 'ـش'. همه تن به تن is an idiomatic expression 'all one-to-one / each individually' (همه quantifies تن; the repetition تن به تن expresses 'person-to-person' or 'in person').
segments.s181-g77: بیاورد is a narrative 3rd-person verb 'he brought' (classical narrative form). صد جامه دیبای روم is a quantified noun phrase: 'a hundred garments (جامه) of Roman brocade (دیبای روم)', with the modifiers following the head noun in the usual Persian order.
segments.s181-g78: همه پیکر is 'every body/figure' (همه quantifies پیکر). از + noun here marks material or ornament ('made of / adorned with'). گوهر و زر بوم = 'gems and gold of the homeland' (بوم = native soil/homeland). Note the copula/verb is omitted in poetry, so the idea 'were adorned' is implied.
segments.s181-g79: هم ... هم is a correlative construction meaning 'both ... and ...'. از introduces the list of materials/items: خز (furs/velvet), منسوج (woven fabric/textile), and پرنیان (silken robe). The coordination with و links the items; the repeated هم emphasizes inclusion.
segments.s181-g80: یکی جام = 'one cup/goblet' (یکی as the numeral/indefinite). پر after the noun is an adjectival/postpositive modifier meaning 'full (of)' so جام پر گوهر = 'a cup full of gems'. اندر میان is a locative phrase 'in the middle / placed amid' (اندر = in, میان = middle).
segments.s181-g249: رخ پهلوان (literally 'the face of the hero') is a possessive phrase where the possessed noun precedes its possessor without an explicit ezāfe (poetic word order). گشت is the past of گشتن 'become/turn' and here means 'became'. ازان is the archaic/poetic compound of از + آن, 'from that/thereupon', and آبدار is an adjective 'wet/moist' describing رخ. Together: 'the hero's face became wet from that.'
segments.s181-g250: بسی functions as an intensifier 'much/many' (classical usage) modifying آفرین 'acclamation/bravo'. خواند is the past tense (3rd person singular) of خواندن here meaning 'utter/voice/call out'. بر introduces the beneficiary/target 'toward/to' (the king), شهریار. The clause therefore reads 'he uttered many acclamations to the king.'
segments.s181-g251: بفرمود is a polite/past reporting verb 'he ordered/commanded'. The sequence places the verb before the subject (خسرو) as is common in epic verse. بسالار ('to/for the sālār' — the chief/commander) plus بار ('burden/charge' or 'task') indicates that Khosrow gave this responsibility to the chief.
segments.s181-g252: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that ...'). خوان is the object 'the banquet/feast'. از خورشگر ('of/from the cook') uses از to mark the source/agent from whom something is requested. کند is the 3rd person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used here in a jussive sense after a command—'let/make him do' or 'he should cause'. خواستار in this construction functions as 'to request' / 'as a requester' (classical usage where خواستار indicates making a request). The whole clause: 'that the banquet be requested from the cook.'
segments.s181-g253: می‌آورد is the imperfect/narrative form of آوردن 'bring' (in epic narrative it functions as a simple past/narrative). و links clauses. رامشگران is the definite/known group of 'entertainers/comforters' (here performers); را is the definite-object marker, and بخواند is the past-tense/single-form of خواندن used as 'summon/call/entertain (by singing)'. Together: 'he brought them and summoned the entertainers.'
segments.s181-g254: وز here is poetic/compressed: etymologically و + ز (and/from) and functions as 'and from/at the' before the following noun phrase. آواز بلبل is a genitive phrase 'the song/voice of the nightingale'. همی is the archaic continuous adverb 'still/then' (used to render ongoing or emphatic action in classical verse). خیره ماند is a compound past: خیره 'dazed/astonished' and ماند 'remained' — 'he remained astonished.'
segments.s181-g255: This is a coordination: سران 'the chiefs/leaders' followed by repeated با 'with' phrases listing companions (با فرامرز و با پیلتن). The repetition of با is a poetic device emphasizing each companion. Word order is straightforward nominative/prepositional.
segments.s181-g256: همی is an archaic continuous adverb (here marking the narrative action). باده is 'wine' and خوردند is the past plural of خوردن; with باده it idiomatically means 'they drank'. بر before a name can indicate dedication or direction 'to/in honor of' (here 'بر یاسمن' — 'to/over Yāsaman' or 'on jasmine'); یاسمن is either the flower 'jasmine' or a proper name used poetically. Together: 'they drank wine to/for Yāsaman.'
segments.s181-g177: This is a protasis ('اگر' = if) with a present/future-oriented clause. 'لشکر آید' uses the classical/present-subjunctive form آید (from آمدن) = 'comes' after the conditional marker. 'نترسم' is first-person singular present with the negative prefix ن- (ن + ترسم) = 'I do not fear.' The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of از) governs 'رزم' = 'from/because of battle' (= 'fear of battle').
segments.s181-g178: This clause continues the speaker's resolution. 'برزم' is an archaic/poetic first-person verb form here (used to indicate going/entering or acting in the event) and functions as the next action after 'I will not fear'. 'اندرون' is an adverbial noun meaning 'within/inside.' 'کرگس' is a concrete noun 'vulture.' 'آرم' appears as a first-person present/optative form ('I let it rest/place it'), used poetically to mean 'I will set/let rest.' 'ببزم' is the final verb-form describing an action of revel/feast in context (poetic root بزم); its exact sense-labeling in the supplied list does not match this usage (see senses mapping).
segments.s181-g179: 'ره لشکر' is a genitive/possessive construction (the route/way of the army). The preposition 'از' + 'برف' indicates 'through/over the snow' or 'because of snow' in poetic usage. 'آسان کنم' is a causative construction with 'کنم' = 'I will make/cause' and 'آسان' = 'easy' (i.e. 'I will make the army's passage through the snow easy').
segments.s181-g180: 'دل ترک' = 'the Turk's heart' (a possessive noun phrase). 'از آن' = 'because of that' or 'from that.' 'هراسان کنم' again uses the causative 'کنم' with the adjective 'هراسان' = 'frightened,' so the speaker says he will cause the Turk's heart to be frightened ('I will frighten the Turk's heart').
segments.s181-g181: 'همه خواسته گیو را' is the direct object phrase: 'all (همه) the requests/wishes (خواسته) of Giv (گیو)'. The particle 'را' marks this definite object. 'داد' is the past-tense verb 'gave'; the subject 'شاه' follows the verb (classical word order permits subject after verb for emphasis).
segments.s181-g182: 'بدو گفت' is an inversion common in classical Persian: 'گفت' (said) with the indirect object 'بدو' = 'to him/unto him.' 'کای' is the vocative particle 'O.' 'نامدار سپاه' is an epithet: 'نامدار' (renowned) modifies 'سپاه' (the host/army), addressed to Giv as a title — 'O renowned commander of the host.'
segments.s181-g183: 'که' introduces a wished-for clause. 'بی تیغ تو' = 'without your sword' (بی = without; تیغ = sword; تو = your). 'تاج روشن' = 'the shining/bright crown' (adjective after noun is common in classical style). 'مباد' is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' (i.e. 'may there never be'). The whole clause expresses a wish: 'May the bright crown not exist without your sword.'
segments.s181-g184: 'چنین باد' = 'so/thus may it be' with 'باد' functioning as an optative particle ('may it be so'). 'و' links clauses. 'بی بت برهمن مباد' repeats the optative-negative pattern: 'بی' = without, 'بت' = idol, 'برهمن' = Brahmin, and 'مباد' = 'may it not be' — here meaning 'may there not be a Brahmin without an idol' (a poetic curse/blessing formula).
segments.s181-g137: چو functions here as a temporal/conditional particle 'when/as' introducing the protasis ('when you are a hero'); the phrase تو پهلوان is a nominative vocative-like address ('you, (a) hero'). یار دشمن is a noun phrase where یار = 'companion/ally' modified by دشمن = 'enemy'. مباد is an optative negative meaning 'may it not be', placed at the end to make the whole line a negative wish ('may it not be that the enemy is your companion').
segments.s181-g138: درخشنده جان is a noun phrase 'radiant/shining soul' with the adjective درخشنده preceding the noun (typical Persian order: modifier before head is also common in poetry). تو is the pronoun 'you' (addressing the same person). بی‌تن is a compound adjective meaning 'without a body/disembodied' and is followed by مباد, the negative optative 'may it not be', producing another blessing/wish: 'may your radiant soul not be disembodied.'
segments.s181-g139: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial 'from then on / after that' (از = 'from', آن = 'that/then', پس = 'after'). به گنجور گفت literally 'said to the گنجور' where به marks the indirect object ('to'), and گنجور here refers to the treasury/treasurer; گفت is the verb 'said' (note: the provided senses list for گفت did not include the simple past-verb sense, so see senses mapping).
segments.s181-g140: که introduces the content of what was said ('that' / marking reported speech) and is followed by an imperative clause: ده جام زرین بیار — ده (ten) quantifies جام (cup), زرین is the adjective 'golden' after the noun (poetic order), and بیار is the imperative 'bring'. از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'from the hidden (store/hoard)', where نهفت functions as 'hidden/concealed (place or thing)'.
segments.s181-g141: شمامه نهاده is a verb phrase where شمامه (musk/fragrance) is the direct object placed before the verb نهاده ('has placed/put') — a typical poetic/archaic ordering. در آن جام زر is a locative/description 'in that golden cup' (در = 'in', آن = 'that', جام = 'cup', زر = 'gold' used attributively). The whole segment describes placing musk into the specified golden cup.
segments.s181-g142: The segment begins with the numeral ده (implied in the full line) governing an elided noun (ten [cups]) and continues از نقره خام 'of plain/raw silver' where از expresses partitive/material 'of/from', نقره names the material 'silver', خام qualifies it 'raw/plain'. با شش گهر adds an accompaniment/feature 'with six jewels' (با = 'with', شش = six, گهر = jewel). Word order is compact and elliptical — the noun 'cups' is omitted but understood from the preceding context.
segments.s181-g143: پر از مشک is an adjectival-phrase 'full of musk' where پر (here 'full (of)') precedes the prepositional از + noun (مشک). جامی is an indefinite/partitive 'a cup' (جام + -ی) and the following ز یاقوت زرد uses poetic ز (short form of از) to indicate material/ornament 'of yellow ruby' (یاقوت زرد = 'yellow ruby'). The sequence lists a cup filled with musk and adorned with a yellow ruby.
segments.s181-g144: ز پیروزه دیگر یکی لاژورد is a compact enumerative clause: ز پیروزه دیگر 'another (cup) of turquoise' where ز = 'of', پیروزه = 'turquoise', دیگر = 'other/another'. یکی لاژورد 'one (is) lapis-lazuli' — یکی = 'one' (a further item), لاژورد = 'lapis lazuli'. The verbs or copulas are omitted (common in poetic lists) and the nouns are linked by the prepositional/partitive ز.
segments.s181-g457: پر از درد is a descriptive prepositional phrase meaning "full of grief/pain" (پر + از + درد). It precedes the verb and modifies the understood subject (he). از آن جایگه بازگشت is a verbal clause: از + آن جایگه = "from that place", and بازگشت (past, 3rd sg.) = "returned/alighted"; the verb follows its locative phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s181-g458: بسوی + definite goal (سراپرده) gives direction: "toward the inner chamber." آمد is the past tense verb "came." The source z-dasht (ز دشت) follows to indicate origin: "from the plain;" this ordering (direction then source) is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s181-g459: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a senior military commander) and is the sentence subject. فرود آمد is a compound past verb meaning "alighted/descended" (فرود = descent/alighting, آمد = came). از پیل مست is a prepositional source phrase: از = "from", پیل = "elephant", مست = "drunk/intoxicated (here probably describing the elephant or the state of the rider)", giving "alighted from the (intoxicated) elephant."
segments.s181-g460: یکی باره is an adverbial compound meaning "at once" or "suddenly" (یکی = one/once, باره = occasion/instance). تیزتگ here functions as the object/target of mounting (likely a proper name or epithet of a mount); برنشست is past tense "sat up/on" or "mounted" (بر + نشست). The clause describes an instantaneous mounting action.
segments.s181-g461: گرازان is a plural/collective noun meaning "boars" or "a troop of boars" and serves as the subject. بیامد is the past tense verb "came." به + پرده‌سرای = "into the curtain-hall/pavilion/stage" (به expresses motion toward/into), so the clause means "a troop of boars came into the pavilion."
segments.s181-g462: This is a paired descriptive phrase: سری پر ز باد = "a head full of wind" (سری = head, پر ز = full of, باد = wind) — an idiom for empty-headed/vainglorious; و links to the next phrase; دلی پر ز رای = "a heart full of counsel/plots" (دلی = heartful/heart, پر ز = full of, رای = plan/counsel). Word order is noun + adjectival prepositional phrase (پر ز ...).
segments.s181-g463: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." رستم is the subject. بیامد is past tense "came." بیاورد is a past/future-subjunctive form used here as narrative present/past meaning "he brought" (literally "he caused to come"). می is the direct object "wine." The clause reads "When Rostam came, he brought wine."
segments.s181-g464: بجام بزرگ is a phrase meaning literally something like "a large be-jâm / large cup/goblet" (here بجام is a noun-formation from ب + جام in older/textual usage), with بزرگ "large/great" modifying it. اندر افگند is a verbal phrase: اندر = "into/within" and افگند (past) = "threw/cast/placed." پی here means "after" or "thereafter/in succession." Together the clause says he threw/placed a large goblet after/thereupon.
segments.s181-g201: This noun phrase lists two items joined by و (and). پرستار is an attendant/caretaker (a court servant here) and آن جامه زرنگار is a definite NP 'that gold‑embroidered/gilded garment/robe.' Note the demonstrative آن precedes جامه, as in classical Persian, marking the robe already mentioned or shown.
segments.s181-g202: بیاورد is a literary/poetic past/perfective third‑person singular of آورْدن used here as 'he/they brought.' The phrase با گوهر شاهوار is a prepositional adjunct (with + noun + adjective) modifying what was brought: گوهر = 'gems/jewels' and شاهوار = 'regal/lofty,' an adjective after گوهر.
segments.s181-g203: This clause contains a poetic epithet plus two verbs. ابر here is used figuratively/epithetically (literally 'cloud') modifying شهریار to give a lofty tone ('the lofty/magnificent king'); آفرین کرد is a light verb construction meaning 'clapped/praised/cried “bravo”' (آفرین = praise/exclamation, کرد = did/made), and و گفت 'and said' links to the following quoted blessing.
segments.s181-g204: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'that saying'). با جان خسرو is a prepositional phrase ('with the life/soul of Khosrow') placed before the predicate for emphasis. خرد باد جفت is an optative formula: خرد 'wisdom' is the theme, باد is the optative particle meaning 'may it be,' and جفت 'paired' (i.e., 'be joined/paired'). Word order here places the beneficiary/possessor phrase before the subject noun and the optative verb.
segments.s181-g205: چو is a temporal/comparative conjunction 'when/as.' روی زمین گشت = 'his face/appearance turned to the ground' (روی = face/countenance; گشت = became/turned). The second چون introduces a simile: پر زاغ 'like a crow's feather'—here describing the change (darkening) of the face/appearance. The simile follows immediately after the verb it modifies, a common poetic order.
segments.s181-g206: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' افراز کوه = 'the mountain's summit/height' (افراز = elevation/height). اندر آمد چراغ is a verbal clause 'a lamp/light came in/appeared' with چراغ (lamp/light) as the emerging phenomenon and آمد the past tense 'came.' Poetic inversion places the source (ز افراز کوه) before آمد.
segments.s181-g207: سپهبد (spahbed, a high military commander) is the subject; بیامد is a literary past form 'came.' بایوان خویش is a locative phrase 'to his iwan/portico/porch' (بایوان/ایوان = iwan), where خویش + ش is the possessive 'his own' formed compositionally (خوی + ش). The verb precedes its locative complement here, a flexible poetic order.
segments.s181-g208: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/went away.' گردان = 'troop/unit' (the subject of رفتند understood), سوی خان خویش = 'toward their khan/lord' (سوی = toward; خان = lord/chief; again خویش + ش = 'their own'). The plural verb marks that those who'd been gathered moved off together.
segments.s181-g289: همی is a poetic adverbial particle giving emphasis/continuity before the verb; چشم روشن is a nominal phrase (noun + adjective) meaning 'the clear/bright eye' and functions as the subject; عنان را uses the accusative marker را to mark the definite object 'the reins'; ندید is the simple past (3rd‑person singular or plural depending on subject) meaning 'did not see'. The clause reads literally 'indeed/the clear eye the reins did not see.'
segments.s181-g290: This clause parallels the first: سپهر و ستاره is a coordinated subject 'heaven and (the) star(s)'; سنان را again takes را to mark the definite object 'the spears'; ندید is the past negative 'did not see'. Word order is Subject Object Verb (typical of Persian poetry), i.e. 'heaven and star the spears did not see.'
segments.s181-g291: ز دریای ساکن is a prepositional phrase 'from the still sea' (ز = 'from'); چو here functions as a subordinating conjunction 'when'; برخاست is the simple past 'rose/rose up'; موج is the subject 'a wave'. The sequence is inverted in poetic style: 'from the still sea when a wave rose.'
segments.s181-g292: سپاه اندر آمد is a straightforward verbal clause: سپاه 'the host/army' + اندر آمد 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'into/in'); همی before فوج فوج adds iterative/emphatic force; فوج فوج is a reduplicated noun used adverbially to mean 'troop upon troop' or 'in multitudes'. Overall: 'the host came in, in successive troops.'
segments.s181-g293: سراپرده is the direct object 'the inner curtain/curtains'; بردند is the past plural 'they carried/took away'; ز ایوان بدشت is a compound prepositional phrase 'from the iwan to the plain' (ز = 'from', بدشت = 'to the plain' in poetic usage). Word order places object before verb, typical in classical style.
segments.s181-g294: This is a causative/experiential construction: سپهر is the subject 'the sky/heavens'; از + خروشیدن ('because of the roaring') gives the cause; آسیمه is a predicate adjective 'distraught/confused'; گشت is the past of گشتن used copulally 'became/was turned.' Literally: 'the sky, from the roaring, became distraught.'
segments.s181-g295: همی again gives an emphatic/continuous tone before the verb; زد is the past 'struck/struck out/made (a move)'; میان سپه is 'into the midst of the host' (میان = 'among/middle of', سپه = 'host/army'); پیل گام is a brief noun phrase 'elephant (پیل) step/gait (گام)', here meaning the elephant's stride — overall: 'an elephant made a step into the middle of the host.'
segments.s181-g296: This string lists instrumental/ornamental details introduced elliptically: زنگ زرین 'golden bell(s)' and زرین ستام 'golden praise' (the repeated زرین 'golden' modifies both nouns); the conjunctive و joins them. The initial token ابا is unclear in the given lemma list (see senses): here it appears to function like با 'with' (poetic or corrupt form) introducing accompaniment, but that sense was not provided in the lemma senses, so its precise lexical sense is uncertain.
segments.s181-g369: نگه کرد is the past of نگه‌داشتن/نگاه کردن ‘to look/gaze’; here the verb precedes the subject in poetic inversion so نگه کرد کیخسرو = “Kay Khosrow looked.” از پشت پیل is a locative prepositional phrase ‘from behind the elephant’ (از = from, پشت = behind, پیل = elephant).
segments.s181-g370: بدید is simple past ‘saw’ and takes the following noun phrase as its object. آن سپه را: آن ‘that/the’ + سپه ‘host, army’ + را (definite/direct-object marker). زده is a resultative participle/adjective meaning ‘struck’ or ‘having been struck’; بر introduces the locus/agentive complement ‘upon/against’, and دو میل literally ‘two masts/pillars’. The phrase can be read literally or idiomatically (poetic ambiguity between ‘struck/leaning on’ or a technical battle formation).
segments.s181-g371: پسند آمدش is an impersonal/stative construction meaning ‘it pleased him’ (پسند ‘approval/pleasing’ + آمدش ‘came to him’). سخت functions as an adverbial intensifier ‘very/strongly’. و connects to کرد آفرین: کرد (past ‘did/made’) + آفرین (an interjection/noun here ‘bravo’/‘praise’) — i.e. ‘and he exclaimed “bravo.”’
segments.s181-g372: بدان is the prepositional phrase built from به + آن (here glossed as ‘to/at that’). بخت is the noun ‘fate/fortune’. بیدار is an adjective ‘awake/alert’ describing that fate; و links the next epithet فرخ‌نگین, a compound adjective meaning ‘fortunate/auspicious, bejeweled’ used as an epithet of lucky destiny. Together: ‘to that wakeful, auspicious fate.’
segments.s181-g373: ازان پس is a temporal sequence ‘after that/thereupon’ (ازان = from that; پس = after/then). درآمد here means ‘came in/appeared/entered’ (poetic verb for an arrival). سپاهی گران: سپاهی ‘army/warrior’ + گران ‘heavy/weighty’ used adjectivally as ‘mighty/important’ (a common epic epithet).
segments.s181-g374: همه = ‘all/everyone’. نامداران is plural ‘the famous/renowned ones’ (نامدار = famed person). جوشن‌وران is a compound meaning ‘those in armor/coat of mail’ (جوشن = cuirass/mail; وران = pluralizing/agentive suffix), so ‘all the renowned, mail-clad warriors.’ The noun group appositive-expands سپاهی in the previous segment.
segments.s181-g375: سپاهی is the subject ‘the host/army’. کز is the relative particle (archaic contraction of که ز / که از) meaning ‘which/of whom’. ایشان ‘them/among them’. جهاندار is an epithet ‘world-holder’ meaning ‘sovereign/conqueror’, and شاه ‘king’. The relative clause says that the world-ruling king was among that host.
segments.s181-g376: همی بود is an archaic/habitual past construction: همی (continuous/iterative particle) + بود (was) = ‘was/used to be / was continually’. شادان دل is an adjective phrase ‘joyful-hearted’ (شادان = joyful, دل = heart). و links the final epithet نیک‌خواه ‘well-wishing/benevolent’. Together: ‘was always joyful at heart and benevolent.’
segments.s181-g153: ببر زد is a past action: زد is the 3rd-person singular past of زدن (“to strike”), and ببر (literally “tiger”) is used adverbially/metaphorically here (“struck like a tiger”). بدین is a short adverbial/particle (ب + دین in the text) meaning “with this / thereby” or “thus,” linking this action to what follows. The sequence گیو گودرز دست places the possessors (the two names) before the possessed noun (دست) in classical poetic word order, i.e. “Giv and Goudarz’s hand.”
segments.s181-g154: میان رزم is a locative phrase meaning “in the middle/midst of the battle” (میان = middle + رزم = battle). آن پهلوان را is the definite/direct object (“that champion” marked by را). ببست is the 3rd-person singular past form meaning “(he) bound/tied”; the subject is omitted and is understood from context (linked to the preceding agents).
segments.s181-g155: گرانمایه is an adjective meaning “precious/valuable,” modifying خوبان (“the good/noble people”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” آن خواسته is a nominal phrase: آن = “that” and خواسته (here adjectival/nominal) means “the desired/requested (thing),” i.e. the requested prize or object.
segments.s181-g156: ببردند is the 3rd-person plural past of بردن (“they took/carried”). پیش وی is a locative/goal phrase “before him / in his presence” (پیش = before/in front of, وی = him). آراسته is an adjective meaning “adorned/arranged,” placed after the verb phrase to describe the state in which the things were brought.
segments.s181-g157: همی خواند combines همی (an adverb giving a continuous/ongoing sense, “continually / still”) with خواند, the past/preterite form of خواندن here meaning “to sing/cry out” or “to call aloud.” بر شهریار indicates the target of the praise (“to/upon the king”), and آفرین is the exclamation “bravo/praise.”
segments.s181-g158: که introduces the content of what was cried (“that/namely”). بی تو = “without you.” مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning “may it not be / let there not be” (a wish that something not occur). کلاه و نگین are the objects (literally “cap/hat and gem”), used idiomatically here for crown/ornamental jewels.
segments.s181-g159: وزان پس is a fronted temporal/continuative phrase ('from/after that, then') that sets the time. به گنجور is a dative phrase ('to the treasury' or 'to the treasurer') marking the addressee. فرمود is a past-tense, honorific/poetic verb here meaning 'ordered/said', and شاه ('the king') follows the verb by inversion — a common poetic order where the subject appears after the verb.
segments.s181-g160: که introduces a reported clause (the content of the king's command: 'that ...'). ده جام زرین is a quantified noun phrase with the numeral before the noun and the adjective زرین ('golden') following جام ('cup'). بنه is an archaic/poetic imperative meaning 'place/bring' (used here as the commanded verb). پیش گاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in the presence of the court' (پیش + گاه functioning together to indicate location/occasion).
segments.s181-g81: نهادند is a past tense plural verb meaning 'they placed / they set' (archaic/past narrative). پیش here means 'before, in the presence of' and follows the verb to mark the beneficiary/location. سرافراز is an epithet modifying شاه and appears before شاه in a poetic adjective+noun order: 'the proud/renowned king.'
segments.s181-g82: چنین گفت is a common poetic formula meaning 'thus said' or 'so spoke' (چنین = 'thus'; گفت here functions as the past-tense verbal report 'said'). The grammatical order is somewhat inverted: the reporting verb (چنین گفت) comes first and the subject شاه جهان follows it, so 'thus spoke the king of the world.' با سپاه is an adverbial/indirect-object phrase 'to/with the army,' marking the audience of the utterance.
segments.s181-g83: که introduces a reported/nominal clause 'that.' اینت is an archaic/poetic demonstrative 'this' (used where modern Persian would have این است/این). بهای سر is a genitive construction 'the price/value of the head' and بی‌بها is a predicative adjective meaning 'priceless / without price,' completing the clause 'that this — the price of the head — is priceless.'
segments.s181-g84: پلاشان here denotes a coarse cloak or pelt (a garment/hide), functioning as the thing that constitutes the 'value' just mentioned. دژخیم is an adjective meaning 'cruel/oppressive' qualifying نر اژدها ('male dragon'), so the whole noun phrase reads 'the cloak/pelt of the cruel male dragon' and supplies the object/measure of value in the previous clause.
segments.s181-g85: کجا here functions in a narrative/poetic sense 'when/where,' introducing the temporal clause. پهلوان خواند افراسیاب is verb+object+subject order typical of older/poetic Persian: خواند (here 'called/summoned') with پهلوان (object) and the agent افراسیاب following, so 'Afrasiab called/summoned a champion.'
segments.s181-g86: به بیداری is an adverbial phrase 'in (his) wakefulness / while awake.' او is the pronominal subject ('he') referring back to the hero, and شود is the present/future-type verb 'becomes' used here in the narrative sense 'becomes / is made.' سیر خواب is a nominal phrase 'sated with sleep' (سیر = 'sated/full', خواب = 'sleep'), giving the resulting state: 'he becomes sated with sleep.'
segments.s181-g87: سر و تیغ و اسپش is a triad of direct objects joined by و: 'head and sword and his steed.' The verb بیارد is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'he brought / he bore' (corresponding to modern بیاورد). چو گرد is a simile 'like a dust-whirl / like a whirlwind' (چو = 'like/as', گرد = 'dust/whirl'), modifying the manner in which the items/person came.
segments.s181-g88: به لشکر گه = 'to the army, at the time/occasion' (به لشکر = 'toward/to the army'; گه = 'occasion/time'). ما بروز is a temporal genitive/possessive construction 'our day' or 'the day of us' where بروز = 'day/occasion.' نبرد is the noun 'battle/struggle,' so the final phrase situates the action 'at the time of our battle' or 'on the day of our fighting.'
segments.s181-g385: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning "when." دید is a simple past verb "saw/beheld." آن نشست is a nominal phrase "that gathering/assembly" (نشست = "session/meeting"). The coordinating و links that phrase to سر گاه نو: سر (here "chief/top" used in composition) + گاه ("occasion/time") together convey a ceremonial or prominent occasion, modified by نو "new." Word order is typical Persian: temporal conjunction + verb + object phrase(s).
segments.s181-g386: بسی is an adverbial intensifier here ("much/very"). آفرین is a noun meaning an acclamation/praise. خواند is past tense "he uttered/recited/ cried" (used with آفرین to mean "uttered acclamations"). بر introduces the beneficiary "to/toward" and شاه نو means "the new king."
segments.s181-g387: This is a noun phrase in apposition: گرازه is the main noun (a sow/young boar) followed by a genitival/epithet cluster سر تخمه گیوگان where سر means "head/chief of," تخمه "seed/offspring," and گیوگان indicates "of Giv / Giv's descendants." The whole chunk names the animal as leader of Giv's line.
segments.s181-g388: همی رفت is a habitual/continuous past construction ("kept going / was going on"); همی marks ongoing or repeated action and رفت is past of رفتن. پرخاشجوی is an adjectival/participial descriptor "aggressive/attacking." The coordinating و links another element (ژگان), here most likely a proper name in apposition; the line compresses epithets and names in poetic style.
segments.s181-g389: درفشی functions as a noun "standard-bearer" (a person associated with a banner). پس پشت literally stacks two postpositions/adverbs: پس ("after/behind") + پشت ("back/behind") to mean "at the rear of." پیکر گراز is a genitive "the body of the boar." The phrase describes the position of the standard‑bearer relative to the boar.
segments.s181-g390: سپاهی is a noun "warrior/soldier" and is modified by two coordinating adjectival/agentive compounds: کمندافگن ("lasso‑casting," an agentive descriptor) and رزمساز ("war‑maker/fighter/warrior"). The و simply coordinates the two modifiers; the whole nominal group characterizes the standard‑bearer as a battle‑ready, rope‑throwing fighter.
segments.s181-g391: A coordinated noun phrase: سواران جنگی = "war‑horsemen" (سواران "riders/horsemen" modified by جنگی "warlike/military") and مردان دشت = "men of the plain" (مردان "men/warriors" with the locative/attributive دشت "plain"). The two groups are linked by و.
segments.s181-g392: بسی آفرین کرد is a simple past clause: بسی "much/very" + آفرین "acclamation" + کرد "made/did," meaning "gave many acclamations." The coordinating و then links the next verb cluster اندر گذشت where اندر is a preposition "among/in" and گذشت is past "passed" — together "and (he) passed among (them)."
segments.s181-g105: که is the clause-linking particle 'that/then' connecting this action to what came before. افراسیابش is the proper name Afrasiab with the enclitic -ش (an archaic/pronoun clitic attached in poetry); the whole functions as the subject. به سر برنهاد is a PP + verb: به سر 'to/onto (the) head' + برنهاد, past tense 3rd sg 'raised up' — literally 'put up to the head', i.e. 'lifted his head.' Word order (subject + PP + verb) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s181-g106: ورا is a directional/object pronoun 'to him.' خواند is past 3rd‑person singular of خواندن used here in the sense 'called/summoned.' بیدار و فرخ نژاد are vocative/adjectival epithets addressed to the addressee: بیدار 'awake' and فرخ نژاد a compound 'fortunate/noble‑born' (فرخ = fortunate, نژاد = lineage). The sequence is 'he called to him: Awake, noble‑born.'
segments.s181-g107: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that very' identifying the person just mentioned. بیژن گیو is the hero's name with a patronymic (Bijan, (son of) Giv). برجست is past 3rd‑person singular 'rose up/ sprang up'; زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the action. Poetic order places the adverb after the verb.
segments.s181-g108: کجا بود is a rhetorical question 'where was he?' در جنگ 'in the battle' marks location. برسان دود is a verbal phrase 'bringing/raising smoke' where برسان (literally 'bring/raise') describes the battle (a relative/participial use common in older/poetic Persian): 'the battle that sent up smoke.' The whole answers the question: 'He was in the battle that sent up smoke.'
segments.s181-g109: بزد دست is a simple past action 'he struck (his) hand' (an idiomatic gesture). و coordinates the next action. آن هدیه‌ها is the direct object 'those gifts' (آن + plural هدیه‌ها). برگرفت is past 3rd‑person singular 'took up / took away' — the same subject performs both verbs, so the subject is omitted in the second clause.
segments.s181-g110: ازو here means 'because of him / at him' (marks the cause or object of the assembly's astonishment). ماند is past 3rd‑person singular 'remained / was left.' آن انجمن در شگفت is the noun phrase 'that assembly in wonder' — i.e. 'the assembly was left astonished.' The phrase order (cause + verb + noun phrase + prepositional complement) is typical of narrative style.
segments.s181-g111: بسی here is an adverbial intensifier 'much/very' (classical use). آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (an exclamation of praise) + کرد 'did' → 'he praised' or 'he exclaimed approval.' و بنشست شاد coordinates the following clause: بنشست is past 3rd‑person singular 'sat down' and شاد is 'happy/joyful' describing how he sat.
segments.s181-g112: که introduces a content clause giving the wish. گیتی 'the world' is the subject; به marks the beneficiary/target ('for/under') Kiškhsrow (کیخسرو) is the ruler named; آباد 'prosperous' is the predicate adjective; باد is an optative particle 'may it be' (a fixed poetic subjunctive). The whole line is a wish: 'May the world be prosperous for Kay Khosrow.'
segments.s181-g393: ازان ('from/thereupon') opens the clause and sets the cause or occasion. شادمان شد is a two-word verbal phrase: شادمان (adjective 'glad') plus the past-tense verb شد 'became' — together 'became glad'. The following که introduces the content/reason clause; بودش پسند is a small verbless predicate where بود (copula 'was') carries the enclitic -ش (ش = 'to him') and پسند ('pleasing/like') is a noun/adjective: literally 'it was pleasing to him.'
segments.s181-g394: This is a prepositional/nominal phrase describing what pleased him. بزین here functions as a verbal/adverbial idea 'with (the) saddle' or 'saddled/on saddle' (classical compressed form). اندرون is a locative adverb 'inside/within'; حلقه‌های کمند is a genitive construction 'the loops of the lasso' (حلقه‌های = 'loops/rings' in the ezafe relation with کمند). Word order is typical Persian: instrument/location after the predicate it modifies.
segments.s181-g395: This segment gives the scene following him. دمان (lit. 'train/hem' — here 'the trailing line/afterpart') + از پسش ('from/after him') marks location/source 'behind him.' زنگه is a noun 'peal/ringing' and شاوران names the agent/group producing it; the whole phrase describes a sound or commotion coming after him. Word order is noun phrase (source) → sound → agent.
segments.s181-g396: بشد is the past/result verb 'it happened/it became' used here to introduce what followed; با 'with' links the preceding sound to the companions that accompanied it. دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave' and و کنداوران joins another group. The clause is a simple coordination introduced by با + noun list: 'it came with the brave and the ...'.
segments.s181-g397: This is a noun phrase in apposition: درفشی 'the standard-bearer' (or 'banner') followed by پس 'after/behind' + پشت 'back/behind' specifying position relative to پیکرهمای. پیکرهمای is an epithet (pīkar-e Homāy) 'the Homa‑bodied' or 'majestic-bodied' — a poetic epithet placed after the positional phrase. The order places the bearer 'behind the majestic figure.'
segments.s181-g398: سپاهی is the collective noun 'the army/warriors.' چو is the simile marker 'like/as'; کوه رونده is a compressed simile 'a moving mountain' (کوه 'mountain' + رونده 'moving'). ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from its place' specifying the motion's origin. The whole line is a simile: 'the army, like a mountain moving from its place.'
segments.s181-g399: هرانکس که is the free relative 'whoever/anyone who'; از شهر بغداد بود is a locative/predicative clause where از + شهر بغداد marks origin 'from the city of Baghdad' and بود is the past copula 'was' — together 'anyone who was from the city of Baghdad.'
segments.s181-g400: This is a restrictive relative clause further describing those people: که 'who' + با 'with' introduces a list of instruments/weapons: نیزه (spear), تیغ (sword) and پولاد (literally 'steel', here 'armor/steel-breastplate') and closes with بود 'was/was equipped' — i.e. 'who were armed with spear, sword, and steel.' The repeated که and با are typical in Persian to stack relative descriptions.
segments.s181-g337: پس is a discourse connector meaning 'then/so'. هر یک means 'each one' (هر + یک). اندر is a preposition 'in/within' placed before the following adjective + noun phrase. دگرگون is an adjective/participle 'changed, transformed' modifying درفش (banner/standard); the phrase literally reads 'each one, in a changed banner.'
segments.s181-g338: This is the main predication: جهان گشته uses گشته (past participle/verb 'become/turned') with جهان 'the world' as subject, meaning 'the world became/was turned'. بد here precedes a color and functions as an intensifying/qualifying element (archaic/poetic), followed by a coordinate list of colors سرخ و زرد و بنفش; together: 'the world was turned [to] red, yellow, and purple.'
segments.s181-g339: تو گفتی که is a reporting clause 'you said that...' — گفتی is the simple past 2nd-person singular of گفتن ('you said'). گیتی همه زیر اوست is a subordinate content clause: گیتی همه 'the whole world' (noun + quantifier), زیر اوست 'is under him' where زیر means 'subject to / under the authority of' and ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s181-g340: This is a parallel clause: سر سروران is a genitive-like phrase 'the heads/chiefs of the lords' (سر = head/top/chief, followed by the plural سروران). زیر شمشیر اوست repeats the locative/agent phrase 'are under his sword' where زیر again means 'subject to/under the control of' and شمشیر او 'his sword' is the locus of that subjection; ست is the copula.
segments.s181-g341: چو آمد is a temporal subordinate 'when he came' (چو = 'when'; آمد past 'came'). بنزدیکی is an adverbial/locative 'near, close to' and it governs the following noun phrase تخت شاه 'the king's throne' (تخت = throne, شاه = king). Word order places the locative before the head noun.
segments.s181-g342: بسی here functions as an adverb/adjective 'much/many' (an intensifier). آفرین is a noun/interjection 'praise/acclamation'. خواند is past 'he/they uttered/recited' (خواند = 'he read/sang/called out'). بر + [تاج و گاه] marks the objects 'over/upon the crown and the seat'; گاه in epic usage often refers to the royal seat/throne (archaic meaning). The clause therefore means 'he uttered many acclamations for the crown and throne.'
segments.s181-g343: بگودرز is an older/formal dative-style prepositional phrase 'to/for Goudarz' (ب‑ prefix marking the indirect object). و is the coordinating 'and'. بر شاه کرد آفرین is 'he made/acclaimed praise for the king': بر with شاه marks the target ('for the king'), کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made', and آفرین is the object 'praise/acclamation'.
segments.s181-g344: This short clause repeats the same pattern for other recipients: چه functions here as a correlative/connecting particle ('and/also/likewise' in poetic usage). بر گیو و بر لشکرش marks 'upon/for Giv and upon his army' (two prepositional targets). همچنین 'likewise/also' reinforces that the same action (praising) applied to them as well.
segments.s181-g49: نوشتند is third-person plural past (they wrote/inscribed). The verb precedes the location phrase here (poetic word order). بر دفتر شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'on/in the king's book/register' where بر + دفتر gives the sense of 'in/on the register of' and شهریار is in a genitive relation to دفتر (the king's register).
segments.s181-g50: همه نامشان is a noun phrase 'all their names' (همه = all; نام + شان = their name). تا کی آید به کار is a subordinate temporal/purposive clause: تا + کی (here 'when' / 'in case') + آید (3sg present/subjunctive 'may come' used to express future/possibility) + به کار (idiom 'into use' / 'to be of use'). Together it means 'so that/when they may come into use' or 'in case they are needed.'
segments.s181-g51: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/said'. It introduces the subordinate clause beginning with کز, an archaic contraction of که از ('that from' / 'that they should from'), which here marks origin ('from the city'). بیرون شوند puts the adverb بیرون ('out') before the verb شوند (subjunctive/optative 3pl 'they should be/go out') — a common poetic ordering.
segments.s181-g52: ز پهلو سوی دشت و هامون is a sequence of prepositional/directional phrases: ز (from) + پهلو (the flank/side), سوی (toward) + دشت و هامون (the plains and the hamun). The final شوند repeats the subjunctive/optative verb for this coordinated directional phrase: 'may go/move toward...'.
segments.s181-g53: سر ماه is a temporal noun phrase 'the start/beginning of the month' (سر = beginning/top + ماه = month). باید که is the impersonal modal 'it is necessary that' and introduces the clause with که. از کرنای marks the source/instrument 'from the trumpet' (i.e. the sound of the trumpet).
segments.s181-g54: خروش آید is a clause 'a roar/uproar should come' with آید (3sg present/subjunctive) expressing expected occurrence. The coordinated clause و زخم هندی درای uses زخم هندی ('Indian wounds' — noun + adjective) followed by درای, an older/polite present form meaning 'have/are possessing' used here like an intransitive verb 'there be / there are' (i.e. 'Indian wounds be present').
segments.s181-g55: همه سر is a compact expression 'all (their) heads' (= everyone). سوی رزم توران is the direction phrase 'toward the battle of Turan' (سوی = toward; رزم توران = the battle of Turan). نهند is an archaic/plural present/subjunctive verb form of نهادن 'to place/turn/set' meaning here idiomatically 'they turn/raise' (they direct their heads toward).
segments.s181-g56: همه شادمانی و سوران is a coordinated noun phrase 'all rejoicing and feasts/festivities' (سوران here = feasts/festive gatherings). The same verb نهند (archaic 3pl) follows and means 'they set up/prepare' — so 'they prepare rejoicing and feasts.'
segments.s181-g161: برو ریز is an imperative cluster: برو (imperative of رفتن) + ریز (imperative of ریختن) = 'go and pour/scatter'. The objects follow the verbs in a simple verb‑object order and are coordinated by و: دینار و مشک و گهر = 'dinars, musk, and jewels'.
segments.s181-g162: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain' placed before the noun. افسری (diadem/helmet) is the head noun modified by the post‑nominal adjective خسروی 'royal' (classical Persian often allows adjective after noun). با کمر is a prepositional phrase 'with a girdle/belt' attached to the noun phrase.
segments.s181-g163: چنین گفت is a reporting clause 'thus said'. The following noun phrase (کین هدیه آن را) is the content of his speech; the relative clause introduced by که will modify هدیه. کین here is an archaic/emphatic demonstrative (classical usage: 'this/that'), not the modern common noun meaning 'vengeance'.
segments.s181-g164: ندارد is the negative present ('does not have' / 'there is no'), here governing the noun دریغ 'reluctance/pity' (ندارد دریغ = 'has no reluctance'). از پی is a prepositional phrase 'because of / in pursuit of' (از + پی), and نام و گنج are the coordinated nouns 'name (fame) and treasure'.
segments.s181-g165: از ایدر 'from here' is a prepositional phrase (ایدر = archaic 'here'). شود is the third‑person form of شدن used jussively or optatively ('let it be / let it become'), and تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / until'; در کاسه رود is the subordinate clause 'may it go into the bowl' where رود is the verb (third‑person present/jussive of رفتن).
segments.s181-g166: دهد is the third‑person present/subjunctive of دادن 'gives / let it give'. بر روان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the soul' (بر + روان), سیاوش is the proper name in apposition, and درود is the noun 'greeting/blessing' received by the soul. The verb precedes these complements as a short, performative clause.
segments.s181-g167: ز is the poetic variant of از 'from'. هیزم 'firewood' is the noun governed by that preposition. یکی before کوه is 'one / a certain' and کوه is the noun 'mountain'. بیند is the third‑person present of دیدن ('he sees' — classical narrative often uses present), and بلند is the adjective 'tall/lofty' modifying کوه (post‑nominal adjective).
segments.s181-g168: فزونست is an archaic fused form (فزون است) meaning 'is more / increases / is abundant'. بالای او 'above it' is a locative phrase, and ده کمند is the numeral + noun 'ten snares/lassoes' (number before noun). The phrase as a whole says that above it there were ten snares (or tenfold snares).
segments.s181-g345: پس is a sentence‑initial adversative/adverb meaning “then” or “thereupon.” پشت گودرز is a locative phrase (پشت = ‘behind’) with the proper name گودرز as its object, and گستهم is the personal name placed before the copula بود (past of بودن), so the clause literally follows Persian S‑locative‑predicate order: “Then, behind Goudarz Gostaham was.”
segments.s181-g346: که introduces a relative clause “who/which,” linking back to گستهم. فرزند + [name] is an appositive pattern: here the following name گژدهم identifies that son, and بود is the past copula — “who was Gozhdaham, the son of Bidar.”
segments.s181-g347: یکی is an indefinite “one/a certain,” and the noun‑predicate sequence 'نیزه بودی' (نیزه + past copula بود) means “there was a spear” or idiomatically “he had a spear.” به چنگش is a locative/possessive phrase (“in his grasp/hand,” with the pronominal suffix -ش), and بجنگ is an adverbial/infinitival phrase “for battle / for fighting,” qualifying how the spear was held.
segments.s181-g348: This is two nominal phrases joined by و with a copula: 'کمان یار او بود' — کمان (bow) is described as یارِ او (his companion), with بود as the past copula; the following 'و تیر خدنگ' is a verbless noun phrase appositionally describing his projectile — تیر modified by خدنگ (an adjective/noun indicating a dagger‑like or short, deadly shaft).
segments.s181-g349: ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' بازوش is 'his arm' (بازو + possessive suffix -ش), and پیکان is the object ‘arrow(s).’ بزندان here functions as a verbal form meaning ‘he/they struck/thrust (into)’ (archaic/poetic), and بدی in this context conveys the harmful/striking action — so the clause reads ‘from his arm he sent/flung arrows (that struck).’
segments.s181-g350: همی is a frequentative/continuative adverb here (indeed/still/continually) placed before the locative phrase در دل سنگ و سندان (‘into the heart of stone and the anvil’) and the verb/verb‑noun بدی indicating the injurious/striking effect; the whole line emphasizes that his arrows pierced even the hardest targets.
segments.s181-g351: ابا is an archaic/poetic particle here introducing the clause (contextually rendered 'he had' or 'he brought'); لشکری is a noun used adjectivally or as an appositive meaning ‘an army,’ and گشن و آراسته are two coordinating adjectives (‘hungry’ and ‘adorned/arrayed’), giving two qualities of that army.
segments.s181-g352: پر از is the fixed pattern 'full of,' followed by a list of armaments گرز و شمشیر (mace and sword). The line repeats پر … خواسته as a poetic intensifier: the second پر with خواسته ('desired' / 'eager') emphasizes that the force is full‑armed and eager/zealous. The whole phrase describes a well‑equipped, eager army.
segments.s181-g321: درفش (subject) + از پس پشت او (prepositional phrase) + شیر بود (copula clause). از پس پشت is an older poetic phrasing meaning “from/behind the back of,” so the line literally says “the banner, from behind his back, was a lion.” بود is the past copula “was.”
segments.s181-g322: که introduces a relative clause modifying شیر (“that/whose…”). جنگش is جنگ + possessive -ش “its/his fight.” The forms بگرز and بشمشیر are compressed prepositional/instrumental phrases (the b- = old poetic clitic corresponding to با/بِ) meaning “with a mace” and “with a sword.” بود is the copula ‘was.’
segments.s181-g323: بچپ “to the left” is a directional adverbial. بر…رفت is the verb phrase: بر (toward/on) + همی (an archaic continuous/emphatic particle here giving an ongoing or simple narrative tone) + رفت (past of ‘go’). رهام is the personal name Roham placed after the verb (postverbal subject, common in poetic word order). نیو appears here as an instrumental/adjunct element; in the manuscript it can mean a reed‑flute (ney) or a dialectal/new, and its exact sense is ambiguous in context — it likely functions as an adverbial/instrumental tag attached to Roham (e.g. “Roham, with a ney” or a brief epithet).
segments.s181-g324: سوی راستش is another directional phrase: سوی = “toward,” راستش = “his right” (راست + possessive -ش). چون = “like/as”; سرافراز گیو is the epithet + name “the proud/triumphant Giv.” The copula is elided here (typical in poetry), so the phrase functions as an equative/comparative clause: on his right (stood) the triumphant Giv.
segments.s181-g325: پس پشت = “behind” (same as از پس پشت earlier). شیدوش here functions as a personal/epithet (though the supplied lemma lists a common noun meaning ‘milker’); paired with یل (“hero”) it reads as a named/epithetic hero. با درفش is the instrumental prepositional phrase “with the banner.” Word order places the location phrase first, then the person (name + title), then the instrument.
segments.s181-g326: زمین گشته is “the earth/ground had become/was turned” (گشته as perfect/participial ‘become’). از marks cause/agent “because of / from,” and شیر پیکر بنفش strings together: شیر = “lion,” پیکر = “figure/body,” بنفش = “purple.” Together this says the ground was made purple by the purple‑bodied/lion‑figure (i.e. the lion image on the banner).
segments.s181-g327: هزار (a numeral, here with an omitted noun like ‘men’ or ‘horsemen’) + از پس پشت = “from behind” + آن سرفراز = “that proud/lofty one.” The numeral governs an implied plural; the whole phrase describes a mass following from behind that eminent figure.
segments.s181-g328: عناندار is an adjectival epithet “stubborn/contrary” describing those followers or the leader; با introduces the accompanying equipment “with”; نیزه‌های دراز = “long spears” (نیزه‑های is the plural with ezāfe omitted in poetic compression). The whole segment characterizes them as stubborn and armed with long spears.
segments.s181-g145: A simple nominal phrase followed by a predicative perfect participle: two coordinated nouns (عقیق و زمرد) + locative/prepositional phrase (بر او) appear before the verb. ریخته is a past participle/past-tense form meaning 'poured/strewn' and here functions as the main verbal predicate ('were strewn/poured upon it'); او refers back to the object named in the surrounding context (the cup).
segments.s181-g146: A prepositional phrase headed by به indicating accompaniment/means ('with' or 'by') followed by two coordinated nouns (مشک و گلاب) and the verb/adjective آندرآمیخته meaning 'intermingled/imbued'. The whole phrase qualifies the previous clause: '…strewn upon it, mingled with musk and rosewater.'
segments.s181-g147: پرستنده‌ای is an indefinite noun meaning 'a worshipper/devotee' (the -ای ending marks indefiniteness). It is followed by با + a noun cluster (کمر ده غلام) showing accompaniment/possession: 'with a girdle, (and) ten slaves' — Persian permits a series of complements after با. غلام is the counted noun 'slave/servant.'
segments.s181-g148: A quantified noun phrase: the numeral ده precedes the noun اسپ ('ten horses/steeds'). The sequence of adjectives/qualifiers گرانمایه زرین ستام follows the noun in this poetic order and describes the steeds as 'precious' (گرانمایه), 'golden' (زرین), and 'praised/renowned' (ستام).
segments.s181-g149: چنین گفت is a formulaic sequence 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'he said'). What follows is a clause introduced without an overt complementizer: کین هدیه آن را که تاو — here کین functions in the text as a demonstrative/identifying word attached to 'هدیه' (though the supplied lexical senses for کین in the data do not match this use). آن را is the definite object with the object marker را, and که introduces a relative clause referring forward to تاو ('that/whose radiance').
segments.s181-g150: A relative/time clause where the copula بود ('was') places the predicate: 'تاو بود در تنش روز جنگ تژاو' = 'the radiance was in his body on the day of the battle.' در تنش = 'in his body' (تن + enclitic ش = 'his body'), and روز جنگ تژاو is the temporal phrase 'on the day of the battle (Tazhāv)' or more literally 'the day of war, [called] Tژاو.'
segments.s181-g151: سرش را is the definite object ('his head' with object marker را). بدین بارگاه is a locative phrase meaning 'to this court' — here written as ب + دین (literary contraction 'بدین' = 'to/into this'), and آورد is the past tense verb 'brought.' The verb at the end carries the main past action ('they brought').
segments.s181-g152: Another locative/goal phrase: به پیش = 'before/in the presence of' followed by دلاور سپاه, a nominal compound 'the hero/champion of the army' (دلاور 'valiant/hero' modifying سپاه 'army'). The final آورد repeats the past verb for emphasis: 'brought (and) brought it before the army's champion.'
segments.s181-g409: This is a list of place‑names introduced by the poetic preposition ز meaning 'from'. The first ز marks 'from Kashmir'; وز here is a fused poetic sequence (و + ز) meaning 'and from' before کابل, and و then links کابل and نیمروز. Word order is simply a series of locations 'from X, (and) from Y and Z.'
segments.s181-g410: همه is a universal modifier 'all/everyone' preceding the plural noun سرفرازان ('the proud/victorious ones'); گیتی‌فروز is a poetic epithet 'world‑illuminating' applied to that noun. In classical/poetic Persian epithets often follow the noun.
