segments.s181-g411: درفشی is 'banner/standard' derived from درفش; کجا is the interrogative 'where' used rhetorically ('where is there...'); چون is the comparative 'like/as'; دلاور پدر is the noun phrase 'the brave father' (دلاور modifying پدر). The phrase asks rhetorically for a standard comparable to that of the brave father.
segments.s181-g412: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. کس here with the negative verb نبودی functions as 'no one' (an indefinite negation). را is the definite/object/topic marker placed after کس for emphasis. ز رستم literally 'from Rostam' (in older Persian used in comparative/relational senses) and نبودی is the past negative 'there was not' or 'was not'; گذر (literally 'passing') is used to mean 'getting past' or 'surpassing.' The whole clause therefore means 'for there was no one who could get past/surpass Rostam.'
segments.s181-g413: سرش is 'its head' (سر + possessive ش). The numeral هفت follows the noun in this poetic line: 'its head was seven' = 'it had seven heads.' همچون is the comparative 'like,' and سر اژدها is the simile '(the) head of a dragon' — so 'its heads were like a dragon's head(s).'
segments.s181-g414: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you.' گفتی here functions as the past tense verb 'you said' (note: the provided sense list did not include the verbal past; this is the verbal reading). ز بند 'from the bond' indicates origin or release, آمدستی is the archaic 2nd‑person past form (آمدی → آمدستی) meaning 'you came,' and رها is a predicate adjective/participle 'free/unbound.' So the clause reports your words: 'you said you came free from the bond.'
segments.s181-g415: بیامد is the past tense 'he/it came.' بسان is a poetic simile marker 'like/as.' درختی here means 'a tree' (in apposition of comparison), and ببار in this context describes bearing fruit or being laden (a poetic predicate); the verb form is compressed poetically to produce 'came like a fruit‑laden tree.'
segments.s181-g416: یکی is an indefinite 'someone/a certain one.' آفرین is the interjection 'bravo/acclamation.' خواند is the past tense 'he uttered/called' (here 'uttered the cry'), and بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'to/upon the king' meaning 'addressed to the king.' Together: 'someone cried “Bravo!” to the king.'
segments.s181-g273: Structure: noun + verb + prepositional phrase (classical order can place subject before the verb). برآمد is simple past 'arose/issued forth'. ز (poetic form of از) marks origin and governs درگاه شاه 'the king's court' (درگاه = royal presence/threshold, شاه = king). The whole phrase means 'something issued forth from the king's court.'
segments.s181-g274: رده (here) denotes a military or ceremonial 'rank/row' and is the grammatical subject of برکشیدند. برکشیدند is past plural 'they raised/hoisted'; بر introduces the target 'بر بارگاه' = 'onto the hall/palace'. The clause reads 'they hoisted the ranks onto the royal hall.'
segments.s181-g275: ببستند is past plural 'they tied/fastened' (here a transitive physical action). بر + پیل = 'on the elephant'; رویینه modifies پیل meaning 'armored/iron-clad'. خم is a noun here denoting a curved/arched seat or hump (poetic/archaic usage), the object that is tied on the elephant.
segments.s181-g276: برآمد again = 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن is the infinitival/masdar form 'to roar' used like a verbal noun, here indicating that a roar began; گاودم (literally 'cow's tail') names a tail‑standard (a heraldic staff) and functions as the agent/source of the sound. The clause means 'the roar of the ox‑tail rose.'
segments.s181-g277: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they placed/put' (here a concrete placing). بر marks location 'on'; کوهه is an emphatic (mountain/high) noun modifying پیل, giving a simile 'the elephant's mountain‑like back'; تخت is the object 'throne' that was set. Word order is verb → locative phrase → object in the descriptive poetic sequence.
segments.s181-g278: This clause centers on آمد 'came' with ببار as a prefixed/intensifying element (poetic) giving a sense of 'came forth' or 'appeared in force.' آن qualifies the following noun as 'that'; خسروانی is an adjective 'kingly/royal' and درخت is literal 'tree' used metaphorically for a lofty/important figure or standard. Together: 'that kingly tree came forth.'
segments.s181-g279: This sequence uses two past verbs in a row: بیامد 'he came' + نشست 'he sat', a common narrative formula meaning 'came and sat'. از بر + پیل is a classical locative expression 'on the elephant' (از بر = upon/back of). شاه follows the verb sequence as the subject (verb–subject word order is frequent in older Persian).
segments.s181-g280: نهاده is a past participial/perfect form 'having placed' (here describing a concurrent action). بسر is the classical fused locative 'بر سر' meaning 'on the head' (word order reversed in poetry). بر z گوهر کلاه is a noun phrase 'a cap of jewels' where ز (of/from) marks material/quality (k͟olāh 'cap', گوهر 'gem/jewel'). Entire phrase: 'having placed on his head a jeweled cap.'
segments.s181-g465: همی گفت is an iterative/perfective phrase meaning “he kept saying” (همی marking continuous or iterative action). شادی ترا مایه بس is a verbless nominal clause with an implied copula (“is”): شادی (joy) + ترا (the enclitic 2nd‑person pronoun written as ت + را, here functioning as the object/possessive clitic) + مایه بس (مایه 'cause/resource' + بس 'enough'), so literally “your joy is a sufficient cause.” Word order puts the predicate nominal after the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s181-g466: بفردا is an adverbial phrase 'on the morrow / tomorrow'; نگوید here is the negative present/ subjunctive form (ن‑ + گوید) functioning as 'will not say' or 'should not say' in this context. خردمند کس follows the verb in inverted order and means 'any wise person' (i.e. 'no wise person will say it tomorrow').
segments.s181-g467: This is a rhetorical question: کجا ... کجاست 'Where ... is?' lists the three names before the copula ست (is). The first کجا introduces the question and the repeated کجا + ست places the copula after the names, a typical poetic word order: 'Where are Salm and Tur and Fereydun?' (proper names are placed in sequence before the copula).
segments.s181-g468: همه ناپدیدند is a simple past/perfect idea rendered as 'all have disappeared' (ناپدیدند = they are vanished/they disappeared). با خاک راست is an adverbial/figurative phrase 'with the earth / into the dust' (با = with; خاک = earth/dust) with راست functioning as an intensifier/qualifier ('truly/utterly' or 'made into dust'), so the whole means 'all are gone, turned to dust.'
segments.s181-g469: بپوییم is a hortative/imperative-subjunctive 'let us seek/go in search of'; رنجیم is parallel and means 'let us toil/suffer' (first‑person plural subjunctive/hortative). گنج آگنیم is another coordinated clause where آگنیم (from آگند/آگن) means 'become filled with' or 'take/obtain' so together 'and let us obtain/be filled with treasure.' The verbs are in a chain of hortatory imperatives in 1st‑person plural.
segments.s181-g470: بدل بر here expresses 'in return' or 'in exchange on/upon'; همی before آرزو acts as an intensifier/collective adverb (poetic use) referring to 'all/the mutual' hopes; آرزو is 'wish/hope.' بشکنیم is the 1st‑person plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us break.' So the clause means 'in return let us break all (one another's) hopes.' The verb is hortatory like the previous verbs.
segments.s181-g471: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end' or 'finally.' زو is the poetic form of از او 'from him.' بهره خاکست means 'his portion/share is ashes' (بهره = portion/share; خاکست = ashes), with an implied copula. و بس ('and enough' / 'and that's all') closes the statement; بس functions as 'merely/that's all.'
segments.s181-g472: رهایی نیابد is a verbal phrase where رهایی (release/freedom) is the object and نیابد (ن‑ + یابد) is the negative present/ability verb meaning 'does not/ cannot obtain' or 'will not attain.' ز او 'from him' is a prepositional phrase, and هیچ‌کس 'no one' appears at the end as the subject: 'No one attains release from him.' The verb is 3rd‑person negative form.
segments.s181-g329: This is a noun phrase made of several modifiers without an explicit copula (poetic telegraphic style). یکی = 'a certain/one'; گرگ پیکر is a compound adjective literally 'wolf-bodied' (گرگ = wolf + پیکر = body/shape) describing the standard; درفشی = 'a standard/banner' (darfash) and سیاه = 'black' (postpositive adjective). The words are strung together in apposition: 'a certain, wolf-shaped, black standard.'
segments.s181-g330: A short locative clause with omitted copula: پس = 'then/after' (here temporal/spatial 'after/behind'); پشت = 'rear/behind'; گیو = the proper name Giv; اندرون = 'within/inside'; با سپاه = 'with the army/host'. Together: 'behind Giv, within (the ranks), with the host.'
segments.s181-g331: درفش = 'standard/banner' as the head noun; جهانجوی is an epithet 'world-seeking' (qualifying the standard or its owner); رهام = 'Roham' (the possessor). The final ببر is an appositional name or epithet of the banner ('the Tiger'), placed after the possessed phrase in poetic syntax.
segments.s181-g332: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; بفراخته بود is a compound of the participle بفراخته ('uplifted/raised') + auxiliary بود (past) yielding 'had raised/was raised' (past perfect sense, personification of the banner); سر = 'head' (literal or figurative top); تا = 'as far as/up to'; بابر = the proper name Babur. So: 'which had raised its head up to Babur.'
segments.s181-g333: پس = 'then'; بیژن = proper name Bijan; اندر = 'in/within'; درفشی دگر = 'another standard/banner' ('درفشی' = standard, 'دگر' = other/another). The clause omits a copula and reads 'Then Bijan (was) within another standard.'
segments.s181-g334: پرستارفش appears as a compound meaning roughly 'the keeper/guardian of the banner' (poetic/concise formation). بر سرش = 'on his head' (بر = on; سر + ش = his head). تاج زر = 'a crown of gold' (تاج = crown; زر = gold). The whole gives an appositive characterisation: 'the banner's keeper, with a golden crown on his head.'
segments.s181-g335: نبیره پسر = 'offspring/sons' (نبیره = scion/offspring, پسر = son); داشت = past 'had'. هفتاد و هشت = the number 'seventy and eight' (seventy‑eight). The phrase follows classical Persian order where possession is asserted by داشت + noun and the numeral follows.
segments.s181-g336: از ایشان = 'of/from them' (indicating among those sons); نبد = archaic negative of 'بود' meaning 'there was not' or 'did not exist'; جای = 'place'; بر پهن دشت = 'on the wide plain' (بر = on; پهن = wide/broad; دشت = plain). Literally: 'Of them there was no place on the broad plain.'
segments.s181-g441: مرا و ترا are oblique pronouns (me, you) with enclitic -ا/ـا spelling split here; they mark persons involved. روز هم بگذرد is a verbl clause where روز (day) is the temporal subject and بگذرد (present/subjunctive/impersonal) means “will pass”; هم adds 'also/too'. The word order places the pronouns fronted for emphasis: 'for me and you the day will also pass.'
segments.s181-g442: دمت is دم (moment/breath) with the 2nd‑person suffix -ت, meaning 'your moment/breath.' چرخ گردان is a noun + adjective phrase ('the turning/revolving sky/heavens'); همی + بشمرد is an archaic present/continuous construction (همی = ongoing, بشمرد = counts/reckons) meaning 'keeps counting' or 'even counts.' Together: 'the revolving heavens even count your moments.'
segments.s181-g443: دلت is دل + the 2nd‑person suffix -ت, 'your heart.' شاد باید places the adjective 'happy' before the modal/necessity باید ('ought to be'); this yields 'your heart must be glad.' تن و جان درست is a verbless clause where درست ('sound/proper') qualifies both تن (body) and جان (life/soul): 'let body and soul be sound.'
segments.s181-g444: سه دیگر ببین is an imperative ('look at three others' or 'see three more'), where عدد + دیگر = 'three other/more.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result here ('so that / in order to'), followed by چه بایدت جست = 'what you must seek' (باید + enclitic -ت 'for you' + جست 'seek'). The whole clause means 'look (at) three others to see what you still must seek.'
segments.s181-g445: جهان‌آفرین is the agent 'world‑creator.' از تو marks the target/beneficiary of pleasure ('with you' / 'about you'), خشنود is the predicate 'pleased,' and باد is the classical optative particle 'may' (wishing). The order is subject – target – predicate – optative: 'May the Creator be pleased with you.'
segments.s181-g446: دل بدسگالت combines دل ('heart') with بدسگال + ـت (your ill‑omened/ill‑fated), i.e. 'your ill‑omened heart.' پر از دود is the polar phrase 'full of smoke' (پر = full, از = 'of', دود = smoke). باد again is the optative 'may'—so this is a wish: 'may your ill‑omened heart be full of smoke.'
segments.s181-g447: چو + past verb (بشنید) forms a temporal clause 'when he heard.' پند is the object 'advice/counsel.' جهاندار نو is a noun + adjective 'the new sovereign.' Word order here places the clause elements compactly: 'When he heard the counsel of the new sovereign...'.
segments.s181-g448: پیاده شد is a light verb construction where پیاده ('on foot') + شد ('became') means 'he dismounted' or 'alighted.' از introduces the source 'from' and باره تیزرو ('the swift bārah/charg er') names what he dismounted from; باره here is 'burden/pack/ mount' used of the riding animal and تیزرو qualifies it as 'swift.'
segments.s181-g193: This is a sequence of predicates and attributive phrases describing the subject: دلیرست is an archaic third‑person singular present/copular form (‑ست) meaning "is brave." The following items are coordinated by و: بینا دل is a reversed adjective+noun pairing (بینا modifying دل = "clear‑sighted/clear‑minded heart") and چرب‌گوی is a compound noun/adjective "smooth‑tongued / flattering." The order (adjective/noun, noun/adjective) is typical of classical Persian poetic compression.
segments.s181-g194: نه is the sentential negation placed before the verb; برتابد is a classical third‑person singular present/imperfect form meaning "tolerates/stands" (here negated: "does not endure"). The rest is a chained prepositional phrase: از شیر ("from/of the lion") + در جنگ ("in battle") + روی ("face"). Word order places the verb before the prepositional complements in this poetic line.
segments.s181-g195: پیامی is the indefinite direct object "a message." برد here functions as a verbal past/prefixed form meaning "brought/took," but the lemma senses supplied do not list the verbal "to bring/take," so the matching sense is not provided in the gloss. نزد افراسیاب is the prepositional/directional phrase "to/at Afrasiab's presence" placed after the verb, a normal word order in classical Persian.
segments.s181-g196: ز بیمش is a causal/prepositional phrase (ز = "because of/out of," بیمش = "his fear"). نیارد is an archaic/poetic verb form (related to آوردن/نیاوردن) used here to mean something like "would not bring/show" in the third person; the supplied lemma/senses do not match this verbal use, so no provided sense fits. بدیده در آب is a locative/phrasing about 'the eye/appearance' (بدیده) + در آب (literally "in/into the water"), functioning idiomatically as "show one's face openly" in context.
segments.s181-g197: ز گفتار او is a prepositional phrase "from his speech / out of what he said." پاسخ آرد is a verbal clause where آرد is a classical third‑person singular present ("brings"/"renders") used to mean "he gives/brings an answer;" بمن is the indirect object "to me." The verb follows the object phrase, consistent with poetic word order.
segments.s181-g198: که introduces reported speech/what was said ("that / saying that"). دانید is second‑person plural present/subjunctive form of دانستن used here in the jussive/optative sense "you may know / you know." از این نامدار انجمن is a prepositional noun phrase "of/about this famed assembly" where از = "concerning/of," این = "this," نامدار = adjective "renowned," and انجمن = "assembly/meeting."
segments.s181-g199: بیازید is an older/perfective verb form from یازیدن (here meaning "opened/seized/grasped") in third‑person singular; in poetic diction such forms may appear without auxiliary. گرگین is a proper name (Gorgin) and میلاد here functions as a name in apposition (Milad); دست is the direct object "hand." The noun phrase orders name + name + object in compact poetic form.
segments.s181-g200: بدان is a compound adverbial/conjunctive element (ب‑ + دان) meaning "thereupon / to that [end]"; here it links the previous clause to the ensuing action. راه رفتن میان is a noun phrase where رفتن is a verbal noun ("walking/passage") modifying راه ("way/passage") and میان specifies "between." راببست is a past tense verbal compound of the object marker را + بست ("shut/blocked"), so the whole clause means "he blocked the way between (them)."
segments.s181-g265: همان is a demonstrative ('that/the same') placed before the noun to pick out the previously mentioned king; شاه is the head noun 'king'; بیدادگر is an agent/adjectival noun 'one who does injustice' (a tyrant/cruel ruler) following the noun as a descriptive element. در جهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the world' that sets the scope and will link to the predicate in the next segment.
segments.s181-g266: نکوهیده is a past-participle adjective meaning 'censured/condemned' used predicatively; باشد is the present/subjunctive form of بودن used here to assert the state ('will be / is'); بنزد (بِنزد) is a preposition 'before / in the presence of' and مهان (pl.) 'guests/visitors' names the audience who reproach him. Together the clause means 'will be censured before/among the guests.'
segments.s181-g267: به گیتی بماند: the locative phrase به گیتی 'in the world' precedes the verb for emphasis; بماند is the present/subjunctive of ماندن 'remain / endure' meaning 'will remain'. از او نام بد is the source + noun phrase 'a bad name/reputation from him' (از marks the source and نام بد = 'ill repute'). The whole order is a slightly marked classical word order: locative + verb + source phrase.
segments.s181-g268: همان refers back to the same person; پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'before God / in God's presence' (پیش = before/in the presence of); سرانجام بد is a noun phrase 'an ill outcome / bad end' where بد qualifies سرانجام. The clause implies 'that same one will have a bad end before God.'
segments.s181-g269: کسی را که introduces an indefinite/relativized person 'one who...' (را here is the topical/object marker used in older/classical style). پیشه به جز داد نیست is a clipped construction: پیشه 'profession/trade' + به جز 'except' + داد 'justice' + نیست 'is not', i.e. 'there is no livelihood/trade except justice' or 'whose trade is nothing but justice.' Be aware that به جز is a fixed compound meaning 'except.'
segments.s181-g270: چنو is an archaic/poetic form of چنان 'such'; در دو گیتی 'in the two worlds' is a classical phrase meaning both this world and the next; دگر شاد نیست literally 'is not happy otherwise/elsewhere' — here the sense is that such a person will not be happy in either of the two worlds. The clause states the consequence: 'such a one is not happy in the two worlds.'
segments.s181-g271: چو = 'when' (temporal); خورشید تابان is noun + adjective 'the shining sun' (تابان follows the noun); برآمد is the simple past 'rose / emerged'; ز کوه 'from the mountain' uses the poetic preposition ز (= از). The clause is a straightforward temporal clause: 'When the shining sun rose from the mountain...'.
segments.s181-g272: سراینده آمد is 'the poet/songster came' (simple past). ز گفتن ستوه: ز here marks cause ('from / of'), گفتن is the infinitive 'speaking/uttering/reciting', and ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary / sick of'. Together this means 'the poet came, weary of speaking' (i.e. tired of composing/singing).
segments.s181-g225: همی is a continuous/progressive adverb (here “kept/continually”); باژ is a noun meaning a war-cry or shout; و simply links items; ساوش here appears as a proper name or a nominative item in the series; بتوران is a compound sense ‘to/into Turan’ (ب- + توران) indicating direction; برند is 3rd person plural present of بردن (“they go/take/carry”), used narratively to mean ‘they proceeded/they went.’ Together: “they kept shouting and proceeded into Turan.”
segments.s181-g226: سوی is a preposition/adverb meaning ‘toward(s) / to’; شاه is ‘king’; ایران is the country name; همی again gives a continuous sense; ننگرند is 3rd person plural present of نگر(ی)ستن ‘to look, gaze, regard.’ The clause means they (continually) looked toward the king of Iran — a simple directional gaze verb following سوی.
segments.s181-g227: فراوان is an adverb/adjective ‘abundant, many’; the printed split ب + دان here together function as بدان (to/at that), an archaic demonstrative/directional phrase ‘at that/there’; مرز is ‘border/frontier’; پیلست is an obscure/poetic term (here listing an element—‘elephants’ or an elephantine force); و links the following noun; گنج is ‘treasure.’ The phrase describes abundance at that border: “many elephants and treasure.”
segments.s181-g228: تن is ‘body’ (here collective sense of people/bodies); بیگناهان is the plural noun ‘the innocent’; از is the preposition ‘from/of’; ایشان is the 3rd-person plural pronoun ‘them/they’; برنج is literally ‘rice’ used here metaphorically as a measure of abundance — i.e. the innocent’s bodies were as plentiful to them as rice. Grammatically the noun برنج is used as the comparative/likening term.
segments.s181-g229: ز is the poetic form of از ‘because of, from’; بس here means ‘so much’ or ‘great amount’; the three following verbs/nouns (کشتن, غارت, تاختن) are gerundive/masdar forms listing actions—‘killing, plundering, and charging/raiding’—and together express the cause or extent introduced by ز بس.
segments.s181-g230: سر is ‘head’ (literal here, ‘they raised their heads’); از is the preposition ‘from/with’ introducing the stimulus; باژ is the cry/clamour (the war-cry); ترکان is the subject group ‘the Turks’; برافراختن is the infinitive/verb ‘to raise, to lift up’ used in a narrative clause meaning ‘they raised/they lifted up.’ The line describes the Turks’ reaction: their heads rose with the war-cry.
segments.s181-g231: کنون is the temporal adverb ‘now’; شهریاری is the abstract noun ‘sovereignty/kingdom’; the printed بایران should be read as به ایران (‘to/for Iran’) — a prepositional phrase giving the beneficiary/location; تراست (literary form of است) functions as ‘is’ (here ‘belongs/is assigned’). Overall: “Now the kingship is (for) Iran.”
segments.s181-g232: تن is ‘body’; پیل is ‘elephant’ used figuratively for great bulk/strength; و links the next noun; چنگال is ‘claw/talon’; شیران is the plural ‘lions’ (metaphor for bravery); تراست again is the copula ‘is/it is (yours).’ The clause is a laudatory comparison: your person has the bulk/strength of an elephant and the claws (power) of lions.
segments.s181-g129: به خم کمر is a prepositional phrase meaning “at/by the bend (خم) of the waist (کمر)”; ار is an archaic conditional particle meaning “if/supposing”; گرفته is a past participle/perfect form “having taken/grasped.” The second کمر repeats the noun as the (poetic) object: literally “if (he) has taken the waist at the bend of the waist.” Word order is compact and poetic—condition (ار + participle) follows the locating phrase.
segments.s181-g130: بدان سان is an adverbial phrase “in that manner/thereby” (بدان = به + دان used here adverbially; سان = “manner/so”); بیارد is an older/poetic 3rd‑person singular verbal form meaning “brings/shall bring” (cf. بیاورد); مر is the old object pronoun “me,” and او را is the definite object “him” (را marks the direct object). به بر is an adverbial phrase “to the bosom/into his arms” (به + بر). The verb governs the object cluster (مر او را) and the final locative (به بر).
segments.s181-g131: بزد is a simple past (3rd sg.) “struck/smote.” دست بیژن is a noun + name construction meaning “the hand of Bijan” (classical Persian often omits the ezāfe in poetry); alternatively, with a different subject, it can read “Bijan struck the hand.” بدان هم is an adverbial phrase “by/thereby also” (بدان = “by that,” هم = “also”), and به بر is a locative/adverbial “to/into the presence or bosom.” This segment sets up the action and the consequent adverbial that links to the next clause.
segments.s181-g132: بیامد is the past tense 3rd‑person singular “came” (here a simple past). بر is a classical preposition meaning “before/unto” or “to” (introducing the person to whom he came), شاه is “king,” and پیروزگر is an epithet meaning “victorious/triumphant.” The clause means he arrived/appeared before the victorious king; the verb follows the adverbial guidance from the previous segment.
segments.s181-g133: به شاه جهان is a dative/benefactive phrase “to/for the king of the world” (شاه جهان = the worldly sovereign). بر ستایش گرفت is an idiomatic classical construction: بر + noun + گرفتن (گرفت here, past of گرفتن) = “to take up [doing] X,” so بر ستایش گرفت = “he began/took up praising” or “he praised.” Word order places the beneficiary first and the verbal phrase after.
segments.s181-g134: جهان‌آفرین را is the direct object marked with را: “the Creator” (literally “world‑maker”); نیایش is “prayer/supplication.” گرفت again is the past of گرفتن used idiomatically “took up/offered” (here: offered prayer). The structure mirrors the previous half-line: object + را + noun (act) + گرفت = “he offered prayer to the Creator.”
segments.s181-g135: بدو is a dative/clitic form meaning “unto him / at him” (i.e., indicating the target of the sentiment); شاد شد is the adjectival predicate + auxiliary “became glad / was glad.” شهریار بزرگ is the subject phrase “the great king.” Classical Persian frequently places the predicate before the subject in poetic lines, so the result reads “the great king was glad at him.”
segments.s181-g136: چنین گفت is a reporting formula “thus he said.” کای is a vocative particle “O” (addressing someone); نامدار is an adjective “renowned/famed.” سترگ is an epithet, here used after نامدار as part of the vocative phrase; its precise nuance is archaic/poetic. The whole segment introduces direct speech addressed to the famed person.
segments.s181-g209: می‌آورد is the imperfective/habitual form (mi‑ prefix + past stem آورَد) meaning 'he would bring' or 'he used to bring.' The conjunction و links this clause to the next; رامشگران را is a definite direct object marked by را (object marker), and بخواند (here without an explicit mi) functions in the same habitual/narrative sense — 'he called/summoned' — a common classical/epic alternation where only one verb carries the mi. The verb خواند can mean 'call/summon' in this context.
segments.s181-g210: همه شب is a temporal phrase 'every night' or 'all night long.' همی before the verb gives a habitual/continuous nuance ('would, always'), and زر و گوهر are coordinated direct objects (gold and jewels) placed before the verb, which is common in Persian poetic word order. فشاند is third person singular meaning 'scatter/strewn,' so the whole clause describes a recurring nightly action.
segments.s181-g211: چو از روز شد is a temporal clause 'when it became day' (چو = 'when'; از روز = 'from/out of day,' شد = 'became'). The predicate that follows, کوه چون سندروس, uses کوه (subject) then a simile introduced by چون 'like' with سندروس (sandalwood/fragrant wood) as the predicate — classical word order often places the verb earlier and the descriptive phrase after it: 'the mountain became/appeared like sandalwood.'
segments.s181-g212: اندر آمد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'came in' or 'entered' (اندر = 'in/within' + آمد = 'came'). خروش خروس is a genitive-like juxtaposition 'the cry (خروش) of the rooster (خروس)' and indicates that the cock's cry was heard; بابر here appears as a poetic/archaic noun meaning a scent or perfume that 'entered' at dawn (this sense is archaic/poetic and not represented in the provided sense list).
segments.s181-g213: تهمتن is an epithet/name (here a proper reference to the hero) and بیامد is the past form 'came' (bi‑am̱ad, narrative past). به درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the king's court/presence' (درگاه = royal court/presence) following the verb, a typical elevated order in epic style.
segments.s181-g214: ز ترکان سخن رفت is an idiomatic construction: ز marks the topic 'about/from the Turks,' سخن رفت (literally 'speech went') means 'there was talk' or 'people spoke' about them — رفت is used in an intransitive, idiomatic sense 'went/was said.' وز تاج و گاه continues the thought ('and of the crown and throne/time'); here وز is an archaic poetic linking of و + از ('and of') and functions to link the next noun phrase to the prior 'talk about...' clause.
segments.s181-g215: زواره فرامرز can be read as a placename + personal name ('Faramarz of Zowareh' or 'Faramarz from Zowareh'); Persian often places such modifiers before the name. با او بهم combines با او 'with him' and بهم (here meaning 'together' or 'to one another') emphasizing accompaniment or mutuality.
segments.s181-g216: همی رفت uses همی to signal habitual/continuous action ('he would go' / 'he accompanied') and رفت is the verb 'went' or 'proceeded.' هر گونه از بیش و کم is a concessive/totalizing phrase 'in every kind/manner, of more and less' (هر گونه = every kind/manner; از بیش و کم = whether greater or lesser), describing the extent or variety of his accompaniment.
segments.s181-g417: دل شاه گشت is a past-state clause: 'دلِ شاه' (the king's heart) is the subject and گشت (past of گشتن) functions as 'became/turned.' از + name gives the cause/agent here ('because of Faramarz'). شاد is a predicate adjective placed after the causal phrase, so the whole means 'the king's heart became glad because of Faramarz.'
segments.s181-g418: همی کرد is a classical Persian imperfective/prescriptive nuance: همی marks iterative/continuous aspect and کرد is the past root 'did' used here to mean 'kept doing / gave.' با او marks the recipient/beneficiary ('to him' / 'with him'), and بسی qualifies the amount ('much' / 'many'). پند یاد is a noun phrase 'counsel / admonition' (یاد here functioning as the nominal element tied to پند); together the clause says he repeatedly gave him much advice.
segments.s181-g419: بدو گفت is a short reported-speech frame: بدو ('to him') is the indirect object, and گفت is the verb 'said' (here used in the reported past). پرورده پیلتن is a noun phrase meaning 'the one reared / raised in Piltan' (پرورده, past participle 'reared'; پیلتن is a place-name modifying it).
segments.s181-g420: سرافراز باشد is an optative/subjunctive felicitation: سرافراز is the adjective 'honored/uplifted' and باشد is the subjunctive/imperative form 'may he be / let him be.' بهر انجمن is a prepositional phrase ('for / at the assembly') giving the sphere in which he should be honored.
segments.s181-g421: This is a nominal equative clause with an implied copula: تو (you) + فرزند (descendant/son) + بیداردل رستمی is an appositive/epithet—رستمی qualifies (of or like Rostam) and بیداردل ('wakeful‑hearted') modifies it. In Persian the copula 'to be' is often omitted, so the sequence reads 'You are a descendant of Rostam, wakeful‑hearted.'
segments.s181-g422: ز … و از … are parallel origin phrases introduced by the poetic preposition ز ('from'). دستان سامی is a genitive construction 'the hands/line of Sam' (دستان = 'hands' here, poetically indicating lineage or agency), and از نیرمی gives a second origin-quality 'and from softness / gentleness' (نیرمی as a quality or origin). These clarify the subject's lineage/character.
segments.s181-g423: کنون ('now') sets time. سربسر هندوان is a frozen poetic phrase meaning 'throughout the Indians / all among the Indians' (سربسر = everywhere/throughout). مر تراست is an older/formulaic clause: تراست is a stative verbal form meaning 'is yours' (optative/possessive sense) and مر is a short emphatic/pronominal particle in older Persian; together the phrase conveys that the territory/people are 'yours' now.
segments.s181-g424: This repeats the possession formula but gives boundaries: ز قنوج تا سیستان uses poetic ز 'from' and تا 'as far as' to indicate extent ('from Kannauj as far as Sistan'), and again مر تراست asserts that this entire stretch 'is yours.' The parallelism (repeating مر تراست) emphasizes full possession over the named territory.
segments.s181-g1: بگشت is a past, intransitive verb meaning 'turned/returned' (verb precedes its nominal phrase here, a common classical order). اندرین is a locative demonstrative 'in/into these' and places the action 'into these (places/quarters)'; نیز 'also' adds that this action happened likewise. گردان سپهر is a poetic nominal phrase 'the revolving/whirling host of the sky' (گردان modifying سپهر); note the ezāfe linking is omitted in verse.
segments.s181-g2: This is a temporal subordinate clause: چو 'when' + prepositional phrase از خوشه خورشید 'from the cluster of the sun' (از = 'from/of', خوشه = 'cluster'), then the verb بنمود 'showed/revealed' and its object چهر 'face/appearance' (object follows the verb). بنمود is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'made appear / showed'.
segments.s181-g3: ز پهلو here means 'from the sides' (ز = 'from', پهلو = 'side'); همه موبدانرا is the object 'all the mobeds' with the object marker -را attached (موبدان + -را). بخواند is a past tense (3rd singular) here meaning 'he called/summoned' (brought them by calling).
segments.s181-g4: سخنهای بایسته = 'fitting/necessary words' (سخنهای plural 'words/sayings' with the adjective بایسته after it — poetic word order). چندی works adverbially here as 'a few / some' and modifies the verb براند. براند is a 3rd-singular past-form of راندن used here in the sense 'uttered/expressed' (poetic usage: verb follows its modifiers).
segments.s181-g5: دو هفته expresses duration 'for two weeks'. در بار here functions as the compound دربار 'the royal court' (written as two words in the line); دادن is part of the verbal phrase 'در بار دادن' = 'to hold/keep the court' (classical phrasing), and ببست is the past tense 'he closed/shut'. Overall: 'he shut the court for two weeks.'
segments.s181-g6: بنوی is an imperative/hortative form 'write!' or 'let it be written' (a command). یکی دفتر = 'one book / a single register' (بنوی takes this as its object). اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within', and شکست here names the place where the book is to be placed (lit. 'in شکست'); شکست in this context appears as a repository/chest/secure place in the archive (a specialized or archaic use), so the whole clause means 'have one book written and placed inside the chest.'
segments.s181-g7: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/said'. موبد is the person addressed/object 'the mobed (Zoroastrian priest)'. به روزی is an adverbial phrase 'each day / daily' (به + روزی), and دهان 'mouth' is used figuratively here ('open your mouth' = 'speak/recite'). The clause sets up a daily action ordered of the priest.
segments.s181-g8: This is a content clause introduced by که 'that'. گویند here functions as a verbal root 'to say/recite' (classical 3rd-person plural or jussive sense 'that they say/that he say'); نام 'names' is the object; کهان و مهان is a paired noun phrase 'the chiefs/kings and the great/guests/nobles' (poetic epithets for leading persons). Together: 'that (he) recite the names of the chiefs and the nobles.'
segments.s181-g361: سپاهش is سپاه + possessive suffix -ش, so 'his/its host/army'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. گردان is 'a troop/division' and کوچ and بلوچ name the groups (here 'Koch' as nomadic element and 'the Baloch'); the phrase literally = 'his army from the troop of Koch and the Baloch'. Word order: possessor-suffix on سپاه, then a prepositional source phrase.
segments.s181-g362: This is a pair of adjectival/participial descriptions linked by و. سگالیده is a past-participle/descriptor ('set upon, assailed' / 'dog-attacked') modifying the following noun جنگ ('battle, fight'). برآورده is another participle/adjective ('raised, brought forth') joined by و, and خوچ ('ram') is the noun it describes; together they form 'sægālīde-ye jang va barāvarde-ye khoch' — two qualities of the troops.
segments.s181-g363: کسی ... ندید with a negative past verb means 'no one saw'. در جهان ('in the world') is a locative phrase. پشت ایشان is 'their back(s)' (پشت + the pronoun ایشان), and ندید is past negative 'did not see'; word order is subject (indefinite 'no one') — adverbial — object — verb.
segments.s181-g364: برهنه is an adjective 'bare/uncovered' placed before the noun انگشت ('finger'). یک adds the sense 'one/even one' for emphasis. ایشان marks possession 'of theirs'. ندید is again past negative 'did not see' — the whole clause emphatically says 'not even one bare finger of theirs was seen'.
segments.s181-g365: This noun phrase links درفشی ('banner' / 'standard') with a following participle phrase. برآورده is a participle used attributively here ('displaying/producing/raised') and پیکر پلنگ is a genitival noun phrase 'the figure/body of a panther' that specifies what the banner displayed; together: 'a banner displaying the figure of a panther.'
segments.s181-g366: همی is a poetic/perfective marker often giving a continuous/intonational flavor ('then/thereupon' or 'indeed'). از درفشش is a prepositional source phrase 'from his/its banner' (درفش + possessive -ش). ببارید is the past form of 'to rain/ to pour down' used figuratively; جنگ ('war') is the thing that 'rained' — literal order is prepositional source + verb + noun object (poetic inversion).
segments.s181-g367: بسی is an adverbial intensifier ('much/very'); آفرین here is an exclamation/noun 'praise, acclamation' used with کردن to mean 'to praise/commend'. کرد is past 'did/made'. بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the king' (بر + object). The construction means 'he gave much praise to the king.'
segments.s181-g368: بدان is the fused form (به + آن) meaning 'at that / by that / thereupon' linking the praising to its cause. شادمان is an adjective 'glad, rejoicing' describing the ruler's state. گردش روزگار is a noun phrase 'the turning/course of time' used here as 'the turn of fate' (گردش = turn/circuit, روزگار = time/fate). Together: 'rejoicing at that turn of fate.'
segments.s181-g313: همی is a classical progressive/explanatory particle marking continuous or emphatic action; رفت is the past form of رفتن (‘to go’) and here describes motion in narrative past. The preposition با (‘with’) introduces the accompanying forces: باد ‘wind’ (literal), then و با links a second instrumental phrase with برز (‘exalted height/highness’) and فر (farr, royal glory). The clause lists what carries or accompanies the motion (carried/borne by wind, highness and farr); subject is omitted (typical in Persian poetry) and understood from context.
segments.s181-g314: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic possessive ش (‘his’) and means “his army”; the possessive is expressed by the suffix rather than a separate pronoun. همه (‘all/the whole’) scopes over سپاهش. غرقه is a predicative adjective/participle meaning ‘immersed/drenched’ and takes the preposition در (‘in’) to introduce the material — سیم و زر (‘silver and gold’). There is no copula; the state is asserted directly (typical Persian poetic predication).
segments.s181-g315: برو here functions as an exclamatory/imperative interjection prefacing a shout; آفرین is the exclamation ‘bravo/praise’ and together آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase ‘uttered praise’ (کرد is the light verb ‘to do/make’). شاه جهان (title + epithet) follows the verb, an inversion frequent in Persian verse: the king of the world is the agent who utters the praise.
segments.s181-g316: که introduces indirect speech (the content of the king’s praise). بیشی is ‘more/increase’ and takes the enclitic personal form ترا (here segmented as ت + را) meaning ‘to you’; ترا together works as the second‑person object/dative. باد is the optative particle ‘may it be’ (wishing). و links فر (‘farr’, royal splendour) and مهان (‘mehan’ here used attributively) — the clause reads as a benediction: “may you have more, and may the farr (glory) of the great be yours.”
segments.s181-g317: بهر is a preposition meaning ‘for/in respect to’ (here ‘for every/each affair’), کار is ‘work/affair’. بخت تو is ‘your fortune’ (بخت + enclitic تو as possessive). پیروز is an adjective ‘victorious’ and باد is the optative particle ‘may (it be)’, so the whole clause is a wish: ‘May your fortune be victorious in your undertakings.’ Word order places the wished state (پیروز) before the optative particle in classical style.
segments.s181-g318: بباز آمدن is a verbal phrase meaning ‘to come/return’ in the poetic register (بـ- prefix + باز آمدن); باد is again the optative ‘may it be’, and the two adjectives پیروز و شاد (‘victorious and joyful’) predicate that wished coming/return. The construction is a compact benediction: ‘May your coming/return be victorious and glad.’
segments.s181-g319: پس ‘then’ is a temporal/discourse adverb; شاه is the title ‘king’ followed by the proper name گودرز and the epithet/second name کشواد. بود is the past copula ‘was’ placing the whole noun phrase as a nominal statement. The order (title + name + epithet + بود) is normal in epic narration.
segments.s181-g320: که introduces a relative clause describing that king. با + nouns (جوشن و گرز) indicates accompaniment/possession in the sense ‘equipped with’ — جوشن is ‘cuirass/armor’ and گرز is ‘mace’; پولاد (‘steel’) modifies گرز. The final بود is the copula in the relative clause (‘who was with armor and a steel mace’).
segments.s181-g425: گر is the conditional 'if' introducing the protasis. ایدونک is a small poetic/colloquial particle meaning 'in such a case' or 'now/then' (an adverbial qualifier). The clause places the verb نجویند (they seek) at the end, with با تو 'with you' before it: literally 'If (in that case) with you they seek war.'
segments.s181-g426: برای‌شان is the indirect-object phrase 'for them / toward them' placed before the verb. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'. کار تاریک و تنگ is the object phrase: کار 'deed/act' qualified by the adjectives تاریک (dark, gloomy) and تنگ (narrow, constricted) — together a poetic way of saying 'a harmful, oppressive act'. The whole is the apodosis: 'do not do such a dark and narrow deed for them.'
segments.s181-g427: بهر جایگه means 'for each place/station' or 'in every situation'. یار درویش is the noun phrase 'a friend/ally of the dervish/poor' (یار = companion/ally, درویش = humble/poor person or ascetic). باش is the imperative 'be' (second person). The order is typical Persian: adverbial/goal phrase + predicate + object + copula.
segments.s181-g428: همه here means 'toward everyone' / 'entirely'; رادبا is an adjective meaning 'generous/noble‑hearted' and modifies مردم خویش 'your people' (the possessive is split into خوی + ش). باش is again the imperative 'be'. The literal order is 'All (be) noble‑hearted toward your people.'
segments.s181-g429: ببین نیک is an imperative 'look / see well' (ببین + adverb نیک = 'examine carefully'). تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that' and is followed by the relative-like construction 'دوستدار تو کیست' = 'who is a friend/adorer of you'. The copula ست (is) ends the clause, as typical in Persian.
segments.s181-g430: This is a short coordinate nominal clause: two attributes (خردمند 'wise' and انده‌گسار 'one who relieves sorrow'/comforter) are asked about the subject 'تو' (you) with the question word کیست 'who is'. The copula ست is again the ending verb 'is'.
segments.s181-g431: بخوبی بیارای is an imperative phrase meaning 'arrange/prepare/adorn well' (بخوبی = well, بیارای = 'adorn/prepare' in the imperative in poetic diction). و links the two commands, and فردا مگوی is a negative imperative 'do not say afterward / do not later claim'. The order places the adverbial (فردا) before the verb مگوی.
segments.s181-g432: که introduces the content clause 'that ...' explaining what one must not say. کژی پشیمانی آرد بروی literally 'wrongness brings regret upon you': آرد is the (poetic/classical) present 'brings' and بروی here functions as 'on you / upon you' (a fused prepositional/particle form). The whole clause expresses consequence: 'that a bad deed will bring you regret.'
segments.s181-g281: Word order: numeric/indefinite 'یکی' precedes the noun to mean 'one' / 'a certain'. 'طوق' is the head noun 'necklace'. 'پر' is an adjectival particle meaning 'full (of)' and takes the following noun 'گوهر' as its complement ('a necklace full of gems'). 'شاهوار' is a postpositive adjective ('regal, king-like') modifying the whole noun phrase — a common poetic placement.
segments.s181-g282: 'فروهشته' is a past-participle/adjectival form (poetic) meaning 'cast down' / 'hanging down' and functions as the predicate for what follows. 'از' marks source ('from the crown'), and the phrase 'از تاج دو گوشوار' gives the source and number: 'two ear-ornaments from the crown'. The clause is verbless in effect (typical in poetry): '...; from the crown there hung two earrings.'
segments.s181-g283: 'بزد' is a past-tense verb 'he struck' (3rd person). The direct object 'مهره' ('a bead/jewel') follows it; the prepositional phrase 'بر کوهه ژنده پیل' ('on/against the ... elephant') gives the place/target. Poetic apposition/epithet: 'کوهه ژنده پیل' links 'کوهه' (literally 'mountain') and the descriptive 'ژنده پیل' ('ragged elephant'), a condensed image for the elephant's forehead/huge head.
segments.s181-g284: This is a second clause: 'زمین شد' — 'the earth became...' with 'شد' as the past copula/verb. 'بکردار' is an archaic/formal adjectival phrase (from 'به کردار') meaning 'in the manner/likeness of' and governs the following genitive 'دریای نیل' ('the sea of the Nile'). Together: 'the earth became in the manner of the Nile Sea' (i.e. the earth took on the quality/appearance of the Nile).
segments.s181-g285: Poetic coordination: the preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از', 'from/because of') is repeated before each instrument/cause for emphasis ('from sword and from mace and from drum and from dust'). The conjunction 'و' links items in the list. 'گرد' here means 'dust' (not the verbal sense).
segments.s181-g286: Two short predicates are packed together: 'سیه شد زمین' — 'the earth turned dark/black' (سیه + شد), followed by the asyndetic nominal phrase 'آسمان لاژورد' ('the sky, lapis-lazuli') where the verb is omitted (a typical poetic ellipsis): '[and] the sky (became) lapis-lazuli (blue)'.
segments.s181-g287: 'تو گفتی' is 'you said' (second-person past). The following phrase 'بدام اندرست' is an archaic locative/predicative expression: 'به دام اندرست' → 'is in the snare / is trapped'; here 'اندرست' = 'is within'. Word order places the subject 'آفتاب' ('the sun') after the predicate, a poetic inversion: 'you said (that) the sun is in a snare.'
segments.s181-g288: 'وگر' is the conditional 'if'/'but if'. 'گشت' is the past/optative form of 'to become/turn' used here in a hypothetical sense ('were to turn / should turn'). 'خم سپهر' is a genitive/noun‑noun compound 'the arch/curve of the sky' (خم modifying سپهر). 'اندر آب' ('into/within the water') is the locative phrase completing the conditional: 'if the arch of heaven were to turn into the water...'.
segments.s181-g89: سبک is used adverbially ('lightly'); the two proper names بیژن گیو are listed consecutively as a coordinate subject ('Bijan and Giv'). بر پای جست is a compact verb phrase: بر + پای = 'onto (their) feet' and جست is the past stem 'he/they leapt' (classical/poetic past), so the whole segment reads 'Bijan and Giv lightly sprang to their feet.' The subject carries over to the next verb.
segments.s181-g90: میان 'amidst/in the middle' introduces the circumstance. کشتن is the infinitive/gerund 'the killing' and اژدها را is a definite object marked by را, so کشتن اژدها را functions as a nominal object phrase 'the killing of the dragon.' ببست is a past form (ب + بست) meaning 'he shut/put an end to' or 'he stopped/closed off' here; grammatically the same subject (Bijan and Giv) performs this action. In classical Persian a past tense form may appear as a singular even with a compound subject.
segments.s181-g91: همه جامه is a noun phrase 'all the garments' (همه as quantifier + جامه 'garment'). برداشت is a past-tense verb 'he took/removed' (here 'took up' / 'took with him'). وان 'that' points to the following item, and جام زر is a nominal phrase 'the gold cup' (جام + زر = 'golden cup'). The sequence lists items he took.
segments.s181-g92: به جام اندرون is directional/locative: به + جام ('to/into the cup') + اندرون ('inside/within') = 'into the cup's interior.' نیز 'also' connects this detail to what was taken. چندی is an indefinite quantifier 'some/several' modifying گهر 'jewels.' There is no new finite verb here: the action (placing/taking) is understood as continuing from برداشت in the previous segment — ellipsis of repeated verb is common.
segments.s181-g93: بسی here functions as an intensifier 'much/very many.' آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase 'made acclamations' i.e. 'praised' (آفرین used as a noun/exclamation plus کرد 'did/made'). بر شهریار uses بر + noun to indicate the recipient/target 'upon/to the king.' The clause is a simple past action: 'he greatly praised the king.'
segments.s181-g94: که introduces a subordinate clause ('because/that/who'). خرم is an adjective 'joyful/happy.' بدی here appears in an archaic/ambiguous form that does not match the provided senses (it seems to function as a verbal or causative element 'caused/gave' in context), so its sense is marked as unknown. تا بود روزگار = 'as long as the days/time existed' with تا = 'until/as long as' and بود = past 'was.' The whole clause gives the reason or circumstance for the praise: 'for he had caused joy while the times lasted.'
segments.s181-g95: وزانجا is a fused particle + locative 'and/from there' (وز = 'and/from', آنجا = 'there'). بیامد is the past 'he came.' به جای نشست is a verb phrase 'he sat in (his) place' (به جای = 'to/into the place', نشست = past 'sat' or 'settled'). The sequence narrates movement and then sitting.
segments.s181-g96: گرفته is a perfect/participle 'having taken' or adjectival 'held'; placed before the noun phrase it modifies the following clause. چنان جام گوهر = 'such a cup (جام) of jewels (گوهر)' = 'a jewel-studded cup.' به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in (his) hand' (literally 'to/at hand'). Together the participial phrase attaches to the previous clause: 'he sat ... holding such a jeweled cup in his hand.'
segments.s181-g473: This is a compact clause: شب تیره سازیم = “we make the night dark” where سازیم is a 1st‑person plural present/jussive form of ساختن/ساخت (classical/poetic word order), with the adjective تیره preceding the noun شب for emphasis. با جام می is an instrumental/prepositional phrase (با + noun) meaning “with a cup of wine.”
segments.s181-g474: چو روشن شود is a temporal subordinate clause: چو + شود = “when it becomes light,” with شود (شد) as the copular/becoming verb. بشمرد is 3rd‑person singular (classical/poetic) meaning “it will count/reckon”; روز پی (literally “the following/succeeding day”) functions as the subject/object affected by that reckoning — poetic word order places the verb before its nominal phrase.
segments.s181-g475: بگوییم is 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive “let us say/let us speak” (used here to propose an action). تا introduces a purpose/temporal clause “until/so that”; برکشد is 3rd‑person singular meaning “he raises/lifts” and takes نای طوس (“Tūs’s ney,” the reed‑flute) as its object. Poetic order places the verb before its object.
segments.s181-g476: برآرند is 3rd‑person plural present/jussive “they raise/bring forth.” تبیره is the direct object here (the exact nuance of this word in this edition is uncertain—likely something raised/announced), and با بوق و کوس is an accompaniment/instrumental phrase “with trumpet and drum” (با marks accompaniment).
segments.s181-g477: ببینیم is 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive “let us see.” تا introduces the content clause (here functioning like ‘whether/that’), and دست‌گردان سپهر is a noun phrase ‘the fickle/inconstant sky/heavens’ (adjective دست‌گردان precedes noun in poetic order).
segments.s181-g478: This phrase reads as a directional/relative clause: the prefix/preposition ب (here effectively part of بدین) marks direction ‘to/toward,’ جنگ is ‘the battle,’ سوی reiterates ‘toward,’ and که introduces the relative clause whose verb یازد (3rd‑person) means “breaks forth/erupts.” بمهر is an adverbial/adjunct ‘with favor/with love.’ Note: دین in the provided segmentation does not match a semantic fit here (likely a segmentation artifact).
segments.s181-g479: بکوشیم is 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive ‘let us strive/endeavor.’ وز here is an archaic/poetic particle (likely equivalent to و از or similar) connecting clauses; in this edition its standard senses do not match exactly. کوشش ما چه سود is a rhetorical/exclamatory question ‘what profit is our endeavour?’ where کوشش ما = ‘our effort’ is the subject and چه سود = ‘what benefit’ is the predicate.
segments.s181-g480: کز آغاز = ‘from the beginning’ (کز = ’that/from which’), بود is the past copula ‘was.’ آنچ بایست بود is a tautological/assertive clause: آنچ ‘that which,’ بایست (classical/poetic) = ‘ought to be/was necessary,’ and the final بود repeats the copula to assert that what was required has always been so. The sequence expresses fatalistic inevitability.
segments.s181-g449: زمین را ببوسید is a normal object-verb order with the definite-object marker را after زمین (’the earth’) and ببوسید the simple past (‘he kissed’). The conjunction و links that clause to the next; بردش here contains the clitic -ش (= ‘him/it’) attached to برد, but the supplied lemma senses for برد do not match the verbal use in context (likely a past/take/perform sense), so the pronoun shows an object attached to that verb. نماز follows as the object/goal of that action (’prayer’).
segments.s181-g450: بتابید is a classical/perhaps poetic intransitive past-form meaning ‘turned’ or ‘bent’ (here ‘turned [one’s head]’). سر (’head’) is the noun that follows and is the thing being turned; سوی + راه دراز is a prepositional phrase ‘toward the long road’ where سوی is ‘toward’ and دراز (‘long’) post-modifies راه. Word order is verb — direct noun — direction phrase, typical of narrative Persian poetry.
segments.s181-g451: بسی آفرین خواند is a short verbal clause: بسی (very/much) modifies آفرین (an acclamation/praise) and خواند is the past-tense verb ‘he sang/recited/uttered’. بر introduces the beneficiary/target ‘the new king’ (بر + noun = ‘to/upon the king’) and نو post-modifies شاه (‘the new king’). Note the poetic inversion where praise precedes the verb.
segments.s181-g452: که here introduces the content of the acclamation or a wish (‘that/so that/that you…’). هر دم is a time-phrase ‘every moment’ (هر = every, دم = moment). فزون is an adjective/participial idea ‘increasing/more’ and باش is the present/subjunctive/optative form ‘be’ used here as a blessing/wish (‘may you be’). چون + ماه نو is a simile ‘like the new moon’; note نو repeats the adjective ‘new’.
segments.s181-g453: تهمتن is a proper name (epithet) and is the subject. دو فرسنگ is a measure phrase ‘two parasangs/farsangs’ placed before the verb to indicate distance travelled. با او is the prepositional companion ‘with him’. برفت appears as a past verb meaning ‘he went off/left’, but the supplied lemma senses do not match this verbal use, so its sense is marked as uncertain here.
segments.s181-g454: همی is an adverb often used in classical Persian to mean ‘continuously’ or ‘all the while’. مغزش is مغز + the enclitic possessive -ش ‘his brain/mind’. از رفتن او is a prepositional phrase ‘from/of his going/departure’ (از + infinitive). بتفت is a poetical verb here best read as a figurative verb ‘burned/was inflamed/was struck’; of the two provided senses the figurative ‘scorch/heat’ fits the image of a mind aflame, so that sense is chosen.
segments.s181-g455: بیاموختش is an archaic/poetic perfect/past form built on آموختن with the clitic -ش (‘him/it’). In classical usage it can mean ‘he taught him’ (causative) rather than the more modern ‘he learned it,’ so here it introduces the taught items that follow: بزم و رزم و خرد (‘banquet/feasting, battle, and wisdom’ listed in parallel). The coordinating و’s simply link the series of objects.
segments.s181-g456: همی again is a habitual/continuous adverb (‘also/likewise’ or ‘constantly’). خواست is the past tense ‘he wanted’ or ‘he wished’. کش in this line is ambiguous/poetic (it can be an independent verb ‘to pull’ or an agentive/derivational element in compounds), and the supplied senses don’t clearly fit the collocation here, so its sense is marked uncertain. روز رامش is a noun phrase ‘a day of repose/peace’; برد at the end is a past verb form meaning ‘he took/carried/secured’ in context, but the provided senses for برد do not correspond to this verbal use and so are marked uncertain as well.
segments.s181-g433: ترا را (ت + را) is the 2nd-person object clitic + object marker: 'to you' / 'you (as object)'. دادم is past tense, 1st-person singular 'I gave'. این پادشاهی is the direct object 'this kingship' that follows the verb. بدار here functions as an imperative 'keep/hold' attached after the object (classical word order allows verb and its imperative or injunction to appear after object); the exact lexical sense of بدار is not represented among the provided sense choices, so its precise dictionary sense is flagged separately.
segments.s181-g434: بهر جای is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for / with regard to any place' (بهر = 'for/toward', جای = 'place'). خیره مکن is a negative imperative: مکن = 'do not', with خیره 'staring/gaping' as the action being forbidden. کارزار names the arena of the action 'the battlefield' (object of مکن understood after the verb phrase). Word order: Persian neg. imperative + verbal complement; the phrase as a whole is an instruction not to gape at the battlefield anywhere.
segments.s181-g435: مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become' (2nd-person prohibition). در جوانی is an adverbial phrase 'in youth' qualifying when not to do this. خریدار گنج is a noun phrase 'a buyer/purchaser of treasure' (خریدار = 'buyer', گنج = 'treasure'); the phrase warns against valuing treasure in youth. Word order: negative imperative + temporal adjunct + predicate noun phrase.
segments.s181-g436: This line is elliptical and somewhat ambiguous in the supplied segmentation: منمای is a negative imperative form 'do not show / do not reveal (to me/others)'; کس هیچ = 'anyone / nobody / no one' (here used as 'anyone'); رنج (both instances) = 'suffering / hardship'. The most coherent reading is an imperative forbidding the display or telling of one's suffering to anyone: 'do not show (me/anyone) any suffering.' The token ببی in the provided sense list does not fit this syntactic role clearly and is therefore treated as uncertain in sense choice.
segments.s181-g437: مجو is the negative imperative 'do not seek'. ایمنی is the noun 'safety / security' (the thing not to seek). در + سرای forms a locative prepositional phrase 'in the house/abode of ...'; فسوس is a noun meaning 'insidious whisper / deceit' so سرای فسوس = 'the house/abode of deceit'. The provided sense list for سرای did not include the locational meaning 'house/abode', so that item is marked separately.
segments.s181-g438: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. گه ... گاه ... pairs two adverbials meaning 'sometimes ... sometimes ...' (both گه and گاه are temporal/adverbial words meaning 'occasionally / sometimes'). سندروسست is an adjectival/nominal predicate 'is sandalwood' (literally 'is made/smelling of sandalwood') and آبنوس is 'ebony'. و simply links the two alternatives. Overall the clause says the place of deceit sometimes appears fragrant/sandalwood-like and sometimes dark/ebony-like (i.e., it can appear attractive or austere).
segments.s181-g439: ز تو = 'from you / of you' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'); نام means 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame. باید is the modal 'must / ought', and که introduces the subordinate clause 'that remains lofty'. ماند is present/subjunctive/3rd‑person 'remain' (used here as the verb of the subordinate clause), and بلند is an adjective 'high/lofty' describing the name. The clause as a whole prescribes that you must leave a lofty reputation.
segments.s181-g440: نگر is an imperative or exhortation 'consider / behold'. دل نداری here expresses absence of heart/spirit: دل = 'heart', and نداری (literally 'lack' or 'you do not have') functions as the predicate indicating lack of heart; the provided senses list treated نداری as a noun ('poverty/lack'), but syntactically it functions as a negative predication here. بگیتی = 'in the world / among people' (locative adverbial), and نژند is a predicate adjective 'base / despicable'. The sentence issues the admonition: remember that one without heart is base in the world.
segments.s181-g41: گزین گوان is a poetic plural/collective phrase meaning 'the chosen ones' (گزینگان); شهره is predicate 'famous' and فرهاد is the proper name; بود is the past copula linking the predicate to the subject. Word order puts the group label before the predicate and name: 'Among the chosen, Farhad was famous.'
segments.s181-g42: گه is a temporal adverb 'sometimes'; رزم is the noun 'battle/combat'; سندان پولاد forms a noun phrase 'anvil of steel' (classical poetry often omits the ezāfe linking word); بود is the past copula, so the clause says 'at times in battle he was (like) a steel anvil.'
segments.s181-g43: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تخم here means 'seed/lineage' (i.e., 'descendants' or 'stock'); گرازه is a name/epithet (literally 'sow' in the lexicon) indicating the ancestor or stock; صد و پنج is the numeral 'one hundred and five'; گرد in epic usage denotes a band/company (an archaic collective noun). The whole phrase lists the number coming 'from the line of G.'
segments.s181-g44: نگهبان ایشان is 'their guardian' (ایشان functions as a possessive/third‑person plural referring back to the band); هم is an emphasizer 'also/likewise'; او را is the definite object 'him' (او = 'he', را marks the object); سپرد is the past transitive 'entrusted/committed'. The clause means 'he (the ruler/person referred to earlier) likewise entrusted him as their guardian.'
segments.s181-g45: کنارنگ is an adverbial/prepositional notion 'beside/alongside'; وز here acts as a connective introducing the following group; پهلوانان is the plural 'champions, heroes'; جزین ('جز این') means 'apart from these' or 'besides these.' The phrase enumerates those standing beside/along with the previously named men.
segments.s181-g46: ردان و بزرگان is a coordinated pair: ردان (epic epithet for those who rout or put foes to flight — 'the routers/valiant') and بزرگان 'the great ones'; باآفرین 'with praise' modifies the coordinated nouns, indicating they are hailed/commended. The structure is a simple coordination finished by an adverbial of praise.
segments.s181-g47: چنان ... که is a correlative construction 'so ... that'; بد here functions as an intensifying adjective/adverb in poetic usage ('so much' / 'so great') and که introduces the result clause. موبد 'the mobed' is the subject of ندانست 'did not know/recognize'; مر is the archaic/object form 'me'. So: 'so ... that the mobed did not know me.'
segments.s181-g48: ز is 'because/of/from'; بس here is an intensifier 'so many/so much' (literally 'enough' but contextually 'so many'); نامداران 'renowned men' is the noun being counted; با برز و فر is a prepositional phrase 'with high rank (برز) and glory/splendour (فر)', qualifying the famous men. Together it explains the reason: 'because of so many renowned men with exalted rank and glory.'
segments.s181-g305: "همی بود" is an older/poetic past-continuous: همی (an adverb of continued/habitual action) + بود (past of بودن) = "he was / he kept being." The prepositional phrase "بر پیل در پهن دشت" is locative: بر + پیل = "on (an) elephant," and در + پهن دشت = "in the broad plain," with the adjectives following the noun in this fixed poetic order.
segments.s181-g306: بدان تا forms a temporal/connective sequence leading to the clause that follows. تا here is the conjunction "until," and سپه پیش او برگذشت is a simple past clause: سپه = "the host/army," پیش او = "before him," and برگذشت = past of گذشتن "passed by." The small element ب (here written separately) functions as a prefix/connector attached in classical orthography; دان in this line does not carry its lexical sense 'to know' but is part of the compound connective.
segments.s181-g307: This is a noun phrase identifying the first man: "نخستین" = "first/foremost" placed before the name "فریبرز." The following phrase "بد پیش رو" is an epithet: بد (adjective "ill/bad/fierce") modifies "پیش رو" (literally "one who is before / front-runner"); together it describes him as a fierce/front-line man. Word order is name + epithet in this poetic diction.
segments.s181-g308: که introduces a relative clause "who/that." بگذشت is past tense of گذشتن "he passed/rode past," پیش = "before/in front of," and جهاندار نو = "the new sovereign" (جهاندار = world-holder/sovereign, نو = new). The clause modifies فریبرز, giving his action in relation to the sovereign.
segments.s181-g309: This list describes accoutrements. The initial form "ابا" here is poetic/contracted speech (functioning as "he with..." = او با) rather than the lexical verb meaning "to refuse"; followed by گرز = "mace/club." Then repeated با = "with," تاج = "crown," و = "and," زرینه = "golden," کفش = "shoes." The construction strings items introduced by (او) با/با to enumerate what he carried/wore.
segments.s181-g310: This short clause describes the banner: پس (then/after) + پشت (back) introduces location; "خورشید پیکر" is a genitive/attributive phrase where خورشید modifies پیکر = "the figure/body of the sun," and درفش = "banner/standard." The implied copula is omitted: literally, "then on the back the sun's figure — the banner," meaning the banner bore a sun-figure on its back.
segments.s181-g311: یکی باره‌ای is an adverbial temporal phrase: یکی = "one/a certain," باره‌ای = "occasion/instant," together = "at one moment / suddenly." برنشسته is a participial/compound form (بر + نشسته) meaning "seated upon / mounted," and سمند = "steed/warhorse." The phrase describes a person who, at once, sat on a horse.
segments.s181-g312: بفتراک is an adverb meaning "apart / separately." بر حلقه کرده کمند is a participial clause: بر + حلقه = "on/over the ring," کرده = perfect participle "having made/placed/looped," and کمند = "lasso/snare." Together: "having (separately) made/placed the lasso over the ring," i.e., he looped a lasso over the ring apart from the main group.
segments.s181-g185: بفرمود is an honorific/past verbal form meaning 'he commanded/said' and introduces the action; صد (a numeral) precedes the noun دیبه 'brocade/silk' and the adjective-like reduplication رنگ رنگ 'variegated/variously colored' follows the noun for poetic emphasis. Word order here (verb → numeral → noun → postpositive description) is typical in epic Persian for rhythmic effect.
segments.s181-g186: که introduces a relative clause explaining what happened to those brocades. گنجور here refers to the treasury/treasure-house as the source/agent, پیش آورد is a compound-like sequence meaning 'brought forward (before [the king])' with پیش carrying the sense 'in the presence of/forward', آورد is 'brought', and بی‌درنگ is an adverb ‘immediately’ modifying the verb.
segments.s181-g187: هم از گنج is a prepositional phrase 'also/from the treasure'; صد دانه is 'a hundred units' with دانه here meaning 'units/items' (a poetic counting word), خوشاب 'pleasant drink' is the noun described, and جست is the past of 'to spring/leap' used here intransitively to mean 'sprang forth/appeared'. The verb at the end (typical SOV ordering in poetry) completes the clause.
segments.s181-g188: که introduces a relative clause modifying خوشاب ('whose...'). آب is 'water', فسردست is an adjectival phrase meaning 'cold‑handed' or 'icy/cold', گفتی here is a parenthetical 'you said' (the provided lexical senses list does not include the 2nd‑person past verbal sense, so that sense is marked separately), and درست functions adverbially as 'rightly/indeed'. The clause effectively reports 'which, as you rightly said, had cold water.'
segments.s181-g189: ز پرده is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from (behind) the curtain'; پرستار here denotes attendants/caretakers (an attendant meaning rather than 'worshipper' in this context) and پنج gives the number 'five' —the word order (noun then numeral) is inverted for metrical reasons— and آورید is the verb 'brought' concluding the clause.
segments.s181-g190: سر جعد is a genitive/construct-like grouping 'head of curl' meaning 'curly head/locks'; از افسر 'from/under the helmet' gives the locus, and شده ناپدید is a compound verbal phrase 'became invisible/vanished' (شده = 'became', ناپدید = 'vanished/unseen'). Word order places the locative phrase before the verbal predicate.
segments.s181-g191: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = thus, گفت = 'said'); the following clause states the judgment: کین/ک‌این here functions demonstratively ('this/that') in the sense 'this gift' though the lexical senses given for کین do not include the demonstrative so the precise listed sense is marked separately. هدیه آن را is 'that gift' with را marking the definite object, and سزاست is a present/fixed predicate 'it is deserved/appropriate'.
segments.s181-g192: که introduces the reason clause 'for/because'; برجان is an oath-like locution 'by the life (of)' or more loosely 'to the heart of', پاکش is 'his purity' (پاک + possessive -ش), خرد is 'wisdom', and پادشاست is a nominal/predicative compound meaning 'is sovereign/king'—together: 'for to his pure soul wisdom is king.' The order places the explanatory clause after the main judgment.
segments.s181-g33: This is an interrogative phrase with کجا fronted ('where'). برته is a noun placed after the question word (here rendered as 'crests/tufts' or 'standards' in poetic context), بودی is the 2nd‑person singular past 'you were', and نگهدارشان is a noun + possessive suffix '-شان' 'their guardian(s)'. The word order (question word + noun + بودی + possessed noun) yields an elliptical question addressing the guardians: 'Where were you, their guardians?'.
segments.s181-g34: به گۀزِ «به گِه/به + noun» here functions as a locative/temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at (the) battle'. اندرون 'within/inside' narrows the location; دست is the noun 'hand' but forms part of the idiom دست بردار(دن) ('to take the hand away' / 'to cease / to seize'), and بردارشان is the imperative بردار + clitic '-شان' 'take/lay (hands) on them' (or in idiomatic contexts 'leave them' when used negatively). Together the phrase gives an instruction/location: 'in the battle, within, (lay) hands on them / seize them.'
segments.s181-g35: چو is a comparative/temporal conjunction 'as/like' (here introducing a comparison). سی و سه is the cardinal numeral 'thirty‑three' placed before the noun مهتر 'chief(s)'. ز + تخم is a poetic genitive 'from the seed/stock (of)', and پشنگ is a proper name (the ancestor). The sequence is a standard Persian numeral + noun + 'from the seed of X' construction: 'as thirty‑three chiefs from the stock of Pashang'.
segments.s181-g36: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whose'. رویین بدی here functions as an epithet or proper name (literally 'iron‑hard Badi' in two words) referring to the leader; شاهشان 'their king' is the head noun with possessive suffix '-شان', and روز جنگ is a time phrase 'the day of battle' (روز + noun). The clause therefore reads 'whose king was (the) Rouyin‑Badi on the day of battle', with که linking back to 'مهتر'.
segments.s181-g37: به گاه is a fixed locative expression 'at the time of'; نبرد is 'battle'. او followed by بدی here places a personal pronoun in apposition to a name/epithet (i.e. 'he, Badi'), and پیش کوس is a prepositional phrase 'before the war‑drum' (پیش + کوس). The clause sets the scene: 'At the time of battle, he (Badi) was before the drum.'
segments.s181-g38: This is a nominal apposition listing titles/roles: نگهبان گردان is a compound in which نگهبان 'guardian/guard' takes گردان 'troop' as its complement (i.e. 'guardian of the troop'), و links items 'and', and داماد طوس is 'the son‑in‑law of Tūs' (داماد + genitive name after it). The phrase names two persons/roles associated with the scene.
segments.s181-g39: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', marking origin. خویشان 'relatives/kinsmen' is the head, modified by شیروی 'lion‑like / brave' (an attributive adjective), followed by the numeral phrase هفتاد مرد 'seventy men' (numeral + noun). The phrase means 'seventy brave kinsmen' or 'seventy men of brave kin'.
segments.s181-g40: که introduces a relative clause describing those men. بودند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they were', گردان is the predicate noun 'a troop/unit', and روز نبرد is the temporal phrase 'on the day of battle'. The clause states that those seventy were the troop at the time of fighting.
segments.s181-g57: نهادند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of نهادن 'to place/put' (verb appears first for poetic force). The direct object سر 'head' appears before two locative phrases: پیش او 'in front of him/present to him' and بر زمین 'upon the ground' — a normal Persian order in poetry where object and locatives precede the verb for emphasis.
segments.s181-g58: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; یک به یک is an adverbial formula 'one by one' where به functions in the 'by/‑ly' sense; خواندند is past 3rd-plural of خواندن used here as 'to call/say/cry out'; آفرین is the interjection/acclamation 'Bravo'. The verb خواندند follows the subject and adverbial, producing 'all, one by one, cried "Afreen".'
segments.s181-g59: که introduces reported speech or an explanatory clause ('that/for'). ما is the pronoun 'we'. بندگانیم is a present copular form 'we are servants' with the plural suffix -یم expressing state. و simply links clauses. شاهی is the predicate noun 'kingship', and تراست is an archaic fused form (ترا + است) meaning 'is yours' (second person possessive + copula), with the predicate preceding the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s181-g60: در ... تا here marks an extent: در گاو 'in (the sign) Taurus' تا برج ماهی 'as far as the sign Pisces' — a poetic astrological formula. تا carries the 'as far as' sense. برج ماهی is a compound 'sign (of the) fish' (Pisces). The closing تراست repeats the archaic 'is yours' predicate for the whole astrological span.
segments.s181-g61: به جایی که بودند is a prepositional phrase 'to the place where they were' with a relative clause introduced by که and the past verb بودند 'they were'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the following noun phrase اسپان یله 'the horses (اسپان) loose/with slack reins' — in Persian poetic order the adjective یله follows the noun.
segments.s181-g62: به لشکر گه آورد is 'he brought to the army-camp' (به + لشکرگه, a compound 'army‑camp/place of the host'). آورد is past 3rd-person singular 'he brought'. یکسر functions adverbially 'entirely/at once', and گله is the direct object 'the herd/flock'. The typical word order here places the destination and adverbial before the verb and the object after the adverbial.
segments.s181-g63: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past form meaning 'he commanded/said'. کان کو is an archaic demonstrative + relative collision meaning roughly 'the one who' (کان and کو function here as older poetic forms for 'that/he who' or 'who/which'); کمند افگنست is a verb phrase 'threw/cast the lasso' with افگنست as past 3rd‑singular (an archaic past form). In short: 'He ordered the one who cast the lasso ...'.
segments.s181-g64: به زرم اندرون گرد is a compact, somewhat archaic instruction: به زرم 'to my gold' (i.e. give him gold / to the treasury), اندرون گرد uses گرد in an older jussive/imperative sense 'be brought in / go inside' (poetic word order places these before the verb meaning). و links the next predicate: رویین تنست 'he is iron-bodied / clad in mail' — رویین (iron-clad) + تنست (archaic copula 'is', with تن 'body' base). The whole segment orders the captive brought in and notes his iron/armoured body.
segments.s181-g9: نخستین (adjectival/ordinal) appears before the noun phrase and marks 'first' in rank. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source group (خویشان 'relatives'); کاوس is the proper name 'Kay Kavus' and کی is the relative/interrogative particle here functioning as 'who' (introducing a relative clause modifying 'نخستین' or tying the clause to the following verb phrase). Word order is typical for classical Persian: ordinal + prepositional source + possessor + relative particle.
segments.s181-g10: صد و ده is the compound numeral 'one hundred and ten' with و functioning as the linking 'and' used in Persian numbers. سپهبد is the noun 'spahbed' (high military commander) serving as the object/designatum. فگندند is an archaic past-plural verb 'they cast/sent' used here in the sense 'they set/placed/appointed' (poetic usage); پی is an adverb/postposition 'after, following' that combines with the verb to indicate the idea of 'setting in place' or 'sending/assigning to follow'. Together the verb phrase governs the preceding numeral phrase as its object/complement.
segments.s181-g11: سزاوار functions as an impersonal adjective 'fitting/appropriate' (introducing what ought to be done). بنوشت here is an archaic verbal form meaning 'to write/inscribe' (classical usage with سزاوار: 'it is fitting to write'); this specific sense (verb 'to write') is not among the provided noun senses, so the listed lexical senses do not match and are therefore not used. نام is the direct object 'name(s)'; گوان is a postpositive element meaning 'of such/like' or 'the like of' and in practice forms 'names of the men/people' — it follows the noun it qualifies.
segments.s181-g12: چنانچون is a comparative/simile 'just as' or 'as'. بود is the past tense of 'to be' (here auxiliary/predicate 'was'). درخور is an adjective 'worthy/appropriate' used predicatively and followed by the possessor/standard (پهلوان), so the phrase means 'was appropriate/worthy of a champion.' Word order places the simile phrase before the copula and predicate for poetic emphasis.
segments.s181-g13: فریبرز is the subject proper name. کاووسشان is کاووس (Kavus) with the possessive suffix -شان 'their/his (of them)', referring to the king associated with that group. پیش رو is a frozen locative phrase meaning 'in front of' or 'before' (پیش 'before' + رو, here functioning as part of the compound adverbial). The whole segment places Fariborz spatially 'before/at the presence of Kavus.'
segments.s181-g14: کجا is the relative/adverb 'where' (introducing the subordinate location clause). بود is the past tense 'was'. پیوسته is an adverb 'constantly/always' modifying the clause. شاه نو is the noun phrase 'the new king' (شاه 'king' + نو 'new'). The order (کجا بود پیوسته شاه نو) is inverted for poetic emphasis: 'he was where the new king constantly was.'
segments.s181-g15: گزین کرد is the verbal compound 'chose/selected' (گزین 'choose' + کرد auxiliary 'did' used to form the past). هشتاد is the numeral 'eighty' and تن is the classifier 'persons' (used with numerals). نوذری is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'vowed' or 'votive' (those bound by a vow or votive attendants). The whole noun phrase is the direct object of the verb.
