segments.s181-g16: همه ... و همه is a parallel repetition meaning 'all ... and all ...' used to enumerate qualities. گرزدار is a compound noun/adjective 'mace-bearing' (i.e., mace-bearers); و is the coordinating 'and'; لشکری here means 'soldiers/troops' or 'military' as a noun. The phrase lists the composition of those chosen: all were mace-bearers and all were men of the host/army.
segments.s181-g233: This clause gives the core necessity: یکی (an indefinite "one/a certain") + لشکری (an army; poetic form) is the thing required, followed by the modal باید (must). اکنون (now) locates the necessity in time, and بزرگ (large/great) is an adjective qualifying the army; word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (adjective after the temporal word rather than immediately after the noun).
segments.s181-g234: فرستاد is the lexical verb 'to send' used with the preceding باید to express obligation (bāyad + verb → 'must send'); in classical/poetic Persian the bare past form serves this purpose. با is the preposition 'with' (accompanying manner), پهلوانی is a substantive/collective meaning 'heroic force / champions' or 'heroism' modifying the army, and سترگ here is a qualifying adjective left after پهلوانی for emphasis (poetic inversion).
segments.s181-g235: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. باژ is a short noun meaning 'cry/rumor/report' (a heralding noise or alarm); نزدیک modifies the verb phrase and means 'near/to the king' (i.e. brought before the king); آورند is a 3rd-person plural verb (present/subjunctival classical form) 'they bring' or 'they report' — together: 'if they bring news/cry to the king.'
segments.s181-g236: وگر is the alternate conditional 'or if'. سر here is the concrete noun 'head' (literal head, often used of a severed head in narrative). ب is the preposition 'to/into' (part of the compound بدین = 'to this'); دین as presented is a tokenization artifact (here part of the demonstrative 'دین' in بدین) and does not carry its usual senses in this line; بارگاه is 'royal court/palace' as the goal, and آورند again 'they bring' — 'or if they bring the head to this court.'
segments.s181-g237: چو functions as a temporal 'when' (classical/poetic use). آن مرز = 'that border/frontier'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'completely/entirely' and modifies the following verbal idea. بدست آوریم is a compound verbal expression ('to gain/obtain' literally 'bring into hand') in the 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative sense here used as a future-oriented clause 'when we utterly seize/take'.
segments.s181-g238: بتوران is a prepositional or locative compound 'to/into Turan' (Turan being the enemy country). زمین here means 'land/country' and is governed by the following verb phrase. بر indicates direction/against, modifying شکست (defeat), and آوریم (1st-person plural) completes the verb 'we will bring' — together: 'we will bring the land of Turan to defeat.'
segments.s181-g239: برستم here functions as a proper-name phrase placed frontally as the indirect object/recipient ('to Borstam' or 'as for Borstam'), a common poetic topicalization. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so', پاسخ is the noun 'answer', آوَرد is the past verb 'brought/said/gave', and شاه after the verb is the subject (V–S order is common in classical Persian poetry): 'the king gave such an answer (to Borstam).'
segments.s181-g240: که is a subordinating/quotative particle 'that' introducing the content of the king's answer. جاوید is an adjective 'eternal' modifying the following noun; بادی (here a poetic/formal word) functions as a noun 'a decree/wind' used idiomatically (in some manuscript traditions this word can be read as باید 'must'), and the second که is a relative/clarifying 'that/which'. اینست is the copula 'this is' and راه is 'the way/path' — together the quoted sense: 'that (it is) eternal / this is the way.'
segments.s181-g169: چنان خواست کان = 'he so wished that...'. چنان (literally 'so/thus') modifies خواست (past 'wished/wanted') and کان is a classical connective meaning 'that' (introducing the content clause). The clause that follows is object of خواست: ره کسی نسپرد, with ره 'way' coming before the subject-like کسی and the verb نسپرد (negative preterite/jussive form of سپرد) at the end.
segments.s181-g170: از ایران به توران کسی نگذرد = 'let no one pass from Iran to Turan.' از ... به expresses source and destination; کسی here is used in the negative sense 'no one', and نگذرد is a negative/subjunctive-like verb placed at the end (typical Persian word order: prepositional phrase + subject + verb).
segments.s181-g171: دلیری از ایران بباید شدن = 'Bravery must arise/come from Iran.' دلیری is the noun 'bravery'; از ایران gives the source; بباید شدن is a periphrastic modal ('must' + infinitive/become) meaning 'ought to become' or 'must arise'—the verb شدن is the infinitive/auxiliary at the end.
segments.s181-g172: همه کاسه رود آتش اندر زدن is a compact poetic clause meaning roughly 'all set fire to (everything)'. همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; آتش اندر زدن is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to set fire in/to'. کاسه رود appears as the object/affected thing (literally 'bowls/portions/streams') and its precise idiomatic sense is poetic/compound here — the whole phrase indicates that everything is to be fired. Word order places the object(s) before the verbal idiom, as is common in classical verse.
segments.s181-g173: بدان تا‌گر آنجا بود رزمگاه = 'Know (this) that if there was a battlefield there...' بدَان is the imperative 'know' formed with the prefix ب- + دان ('know'); تا‌گر is a conditional 'if/if indeed'; آنجا بود رزمگاه is a simple clause 'there was a battlefield' (locative آنجا + verb بود + predicate رزمگاه).
segments.s181-g174: پس هیزم اندر نماند سپاه = 'then no firewood remained for the army.' پس is a connective 'then/therefore'; هیزم اندر (in/within) indicates the thing/place concerned; نماند is the verb 'did not remain/would not remain' and سپاه ('the army/host') appears at the end as the affected party. In this conditional context the verb carries a prospective/modal sense ('would not be left').
segments.s181-g175: همان گیو گفت این شکار منست = 'Then/thereupon Giv said, “This prey is mine.”' همان is a demonstrative/adverb 'that very/then'; گیو is the speaker's name; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (in epic Persian it appears as گفت); این شکار is the noun phrase 'this prey/quarry'; منست is an archaic predicative copula meaning 'is (mine)/is fitting to me'—here declaring ownership or claim.
segments.s181-g176: برافروختن کوه کار منست = 'To kindle/ignite the mountain is my work/task.' برافروختن is an infinitival verbal noun 'to kindle/set alight'; کوه is its object 'the mountain'; کار منست is a predicate 'is my task/is appropriate to me'—so the infinitive phrase functions as the subject of the copula.
segments.s181-g241: ببین is an imperative ('look/consider'). The clause تا سپه چند باید بکار is an indirect interrogative introduced by تا + چند meaning 'how many'; سپه is the noun 'army' and باید (modal) + the bare form بکار expresses necessity ('must be employed' — باید governs the following infinitive-like form). Word order is typical Persian: imperative + indirect question giving what to examine.
segments.s181-g242: تو is an emphatic second-person subject (addressing 'you'), and بگزین is the second-person singular imperative 'choose'. از این لشکر نامدار is a prepositional object 'from this renowned host', with the adjective نامدار following the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s181-g243: زمینی is a noun ('a land/territory') followed by a relative clause introduced by که ('that/which'). پیوسته is an adverb 'continuously/always' modifying مرز 'border/frontier'. تست here is an archaic copular/possessive form (= 'تو است' / 'is yours') and functions as the predicate of the relative clause: 'a land whose border is continually yours.'
segments.s181-g244: بهای زمین is the noun phrase 'the value/price of the land' (بهای governing زمین). درخور is an adjective 'worthy/deserving' and ارز is a noun 'worth/esteem'; تست again is the archaic copula ('is' / 'is yours' depending on context), so the clause states that the land's value is fitting for esteem or praise.
segments.s181-g245: فرامرز را uses را to mark the (definite/beneficiary) noun phrase — here Farāmarz is the person for whom the soldiers are provided. ده سپاهی گران is a noun phrase 'ten heavy/precious (i.e. valuable or valiant) soldiers' with the numeral preceding the noun and the adjective گران following it.
segments.s181-g246: چنان چون is an emphatic comparative 'such as / just as' and بباید here means 'as is fitting' (a modal/optative sense: 'as ought to be'). ز introduces the source/selection 'from', so ز جنگ‌آوران means 'from among the warriors' — the whole phrase qualifies the ten soldiers as 'those fitting from the warriors.'
segments.s181-g247: گشاده شود is a passive/impersonal verbal phrase 'be opened / be made wide' (شود = passive or auxiliary 'become'). کار is the subject 'the affair/work', and بر دست اوی is a locative/benefactive phrase 'in his hand / under his control' — اوی is an archaic third-person clitic meaning 'his/he'. The line thus prays that the affair prosper under his control.
segments.s181-g248: بکام is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'to (one's) desire' or 'to the satisfaction of'; نهنگان here is used metaphorically ('great ones' / powerful figures) rather than literal whales; رسد is a present subjunctive/indicative 'reach / attain' (here 'may ... attain'), and اوی is the archaic third-person clitic 'his/they of his'. The clause therefore says 'may sixty of his attain the wish of the great' — a poetic blessing/aspiration.
segments.s181-g17: This is a nominal apposition: the proper name زرسپ followed by the title سپهبد and the noun نگهدارشان (keeper/guardian) with the 3pl possessive suffix -شان. Word order is Name + Title + Noun+possessive, i.e. “Zarasp, the spahbed, their guardian.”
segments.s181-g18: A relative clause introduced by که (who/that). بردی is an older/persianate past form meaning ‘took’ (here 3rd‑person past: ‘he took’ / ‘he assumed’). به هر کار is an adverbial prepositional phrase “in/for every affair,” and تیمارشان is the object with the 3pl possessive ‘their care’ — together: ‘who took care of them in every matter.’
segments.s181-g19: Another relative/content clause with که omitted in sense of continuation: تاج کیان is a genitive phrase ‘the crown of the Kayan (the royal house)’. بود is the past copula ‘was’. و + فرزند طوس appends a second predicate ‘and (was) a son/descendant of Tūs.’
segments.s181-g20: A verbless nominal continuation relying on the previous بود: خداوند here is an epithet ‘lord/master (of)’, followed by a series of nouns joined by و (شمشیر و گوپال و کوس) — the things of which he is lord. The copula ‘was’ is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s181-g21: سه دیگر ‘three others’ is the subject phrase. چو here is the archaic/classical particle meaning ‘like’ or ‘such as’, introducing the names گودرز and کشواد in apposition. بود is the past copula ‘were/was’ agreeing with the predication (classical narrative often allows this succinct appositional style).
segments.s181-g22: A relative clause introduced by که: لشکر is the subject ‘the army/host’. به رای وی is a prepositional phrase ‘by/according to his counsel’ (به + رای + the genitive pronoun وی = ‘his plan/counsel’). آباد is a predicate adjective ‘prosperous/ flourishing’, and بود is the past copula ‘was’ → ‘whose army, by his counsel, was prosperous.’
segments.s181-g23: نبیره پسر is a nominal phrase (‘scions/descendants, sons’) emphasizing lineage; داشت is the past verb ‘had’. The numeral هفتاد و هشت (seventy‑eight) follows the verb as the object (a typical archaic/poetic word order): ‘he had seventy‑eight sons/scions.’
segments.s181-g24: Two coordinated noun+locative phrases describing those sons: دلیران کوه ‘braves/valiant ones of the mountain’ and سواران دشت ‘horsemen/riders of the plain’. The nouns are qualified by the following place‑nouns (no ezafe written in epic diction), joined by و.
segments.s181-g113: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces a subordinate/purposive clause ('that/so that'). با کمر is an instrumental phrase 'with the girdle/belt' (کمر here = girdle/belt) and the numeral+noun ده غلام ('ten servants') follows in the normal Persian order (numeral before noun); the clause is left open (no finite verb here) because the command continues in the next segment.
segments.s181-g114: ده اسپ is a simple numeral phrase 'ten horses' (numeral precedes noun). گزیده is a post-nominal adjective/participle 'chosen/selected' modifying اسپ. به + زرین ستام is an adverbial/instrumental phrase: به here functions like 'by/with' (instrumental/qualifying), زرین is the adjective 'golden' and ستام (used poetically) is rendered 'praise' (so the phrase qualifies how they are adorned). Together the noun phrase describes the ten selected steeds and how they are to be presented.
segments.s181-g115: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. پوشیده is the adjective 'covered/veiled', and رویان here denotes the group described (contextually 'the veiled-faced / veiled ones'); ده آراسته ('ten adorned') indicates the numeral (ده) applies to آراسته, so the whole phrase means 'ten adorned ones from the veiled-faced'. Word order is typical of Persian modifiers: preposition + modifier + head + numeral/adjective.
segments.s181-g116: بیاورد is a present/subjunctive form of 'bring' used here in the dependent clause introduced earlier by تا (it renders the commanded action: 'that ... bring'); موبد is the agent/subject 'the mobad (Zoroastrian priest)' appearing after the verb (poetic word order). چنین خواسته is a nominal phrase 'thus requested/what was so asked' (چنین = 'thus/so', خواسته = 'requested/what was wished'), and together they indicate the mobad performed the bringing as requested.
segments.s181-g117: چنین 'thus' opens the reported speech. گفت here is the verb 'said/spoke' but the provided noun sense list does not include that verbal sense (so index -1 is used). بیدار 'awake/alert' modifies شاه 'king' ('the alert king'), and رمه ('herd/throng') follows in poetic apposition or as the addressee ('to the throng'). Word order (verb before subject/object) is typical of elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s181-g118: که introduces the content/relative clause 'that/which'. اسپان is the plural 'horses' (or cavalry); و is the coordinating 'and'; این خوبرویان همه is a noun phrase 'these fair/beautiful-faced ones, all of them' (این = 'these', خوبرویان = 'the beautiful/fair-faced ones', همه = 'all'), so the clause begins to state what the king said about the horses and those beauties.
segments.s181-g119: کسی را is the indefinite 'someone' with the object/topic marker را (used poetically). که introduces the subordinate clause 'who/that'. چون سر بپیچد is a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if his head turns' where چون = 'when/as/if' and بپیچد is the subjunctive/present form 'turns/turns aside'. تژاو is a noun meaning 'radiance/brightness' modifying the manner ('when his head turns with radiance'). The relative clause describes the person referred to by کسی.
segments.s181-g120: سزد‌گر is an agentive noun 'one who deems fit / who finds it appropriate' and functions as the predicate/role in this line. ندارد is the negative present 'does not have' (here used existentially/negatively). دل شیر گاو is a genitival apposition: دل 'heart', شیر 'lion' and گاو 'cow' provide contrasting imagery — the line says that someone so described lacks the heart of a lion and instead has a cow's heart; word order places the emotive nouns after دل for poetic effect.
segments.s181-g353: This nominal phrase begins with the indefinite/indefinite-like marker 'یکی' (a certain / one), followed by the compound epithet 'ماه‌پیکر' (literally 'moon-faced') modifying 'درفش' (standard/banner). The prepositional 'از' ('from') attaches to the following noun 'برش' (here written together) to indicate source or location ('from/by his side'); word order is determiner → modifier → head → prepositional phrase.
segments.s181-g354: 'بابر' is the agent/name who performs the verb phrase. 'اندر' is the locative/prepositional particle 'in/among' that here helps introduce the action; 'آورده' is a past participle/compound past meaning 'brought' (used as main verbal idea); 'تابان' is an adjectival participle 'shining' qualifying the following noun; 'سرش' is a possessive noun: 'سر' (head/top) + enclitic 'ش' (his/its). The clause literally reads 'Babar brought in, shining its head'—i.e. he brought it in with its head shining.
segments.s181-g355: 'همی' is the frequentative/adverbial particle here ('kept on / was continuously'); 'خواند' is the past-tense of خواندن used as 'sang/uttered' (here: proclaimed/acclaimed); 'بر' with a following noun marks the indirect object or target ('to' / 'toward') so 'بر شهریار' = 'to the king'; 'آفرین' is the vocative/acclamation ('bravo' / 'acclaim') that is being uttered. Word order: adverb → verb → prepositional target → interjection.
segments.s181-g356: 'ازو' ('from/ because of it') introduces the cause or source of emotion; 'شاد' is predicative adjective 'glad/happy'; 'شد' is the past-tense copula 'became/was made'; 'شاه' is 'king' and 'ایران‌زمین' is the compound 'the land of Iran' (an epithet for the Iranian sovereign). The clause means that the Shah was made glad by that acclamation.
segments.s181-g357: 'پس' is a sequential particle 'then/after that'. 'گستهم' is a first-person past form ('I sent forth / I went about / I wandered' depending on the lexical choice) and carries the narrative subject to first person here. 'اشکش' in the line functions as a proper name/term of someone or something sent forth (the listed dictionary senses do not include a proper-name reading, so this token is treated as outside the provided senses). 'تیزگوش' is an epithet 'sharp-eared/keen-eared' modifying that name. Together: 'Then I sent forth Ashkash, the sharp‑eared.'
segments.s181-g358: 'که' introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/which/that'); 'با' + noun means 'with/possessed of' (here 'with strength and heart'), so 'با زور و دل بود' = 'he was endowed with strength and heart'—'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The second coordinate 'و با مغز و هوش' repeats the 'with' construction to add 'brain and intellect' as further attributes. The sequence lists personal qualities (physical force, courage/heart, and mind).
segments.s181-g359: The phrase begins with indefinite 'یکی' ('a certain'); 'گرزدار' is a compound noun/adjective 'mace‑bearer' (one who bears a mace); 'از' means 'of/from' marking origin; 'نژاد' is 'lineage/stock'; 'همای' here names the ancestor/source (Homay), so 'از نژاد همای' = 'of Homay's lineage.' Word order: indefinite → role/epithet → origin marker → lineage → ancestor name.
segments.s181-g360: 'براهی' ('on the way', 'on the road') gives the scene/setting. 'که' introduces a relative clause describing that mace-bearer. 'جستیش' is formed from a root meaning 'to spring/jump' (here used as 'his spring/leap' or a verbal idea of leaping) and is followed by 'بودی' (past auxiliary 'was') in older narrative morphology. 'بپای' here expresses 'to lie in wait / to watch / to stand ready' (an older/dialectal verb form). Altogether the clause says that he leapt on the road and lay in wait.
